coredata.dc:title: Flat sheet thin film composite forward osmosis membranes for water treatment and purification: A review on modification techniques and concepts

Edokali, Mohamed

coredata.dc:description: Forward osmosis (FO) is an extensively studied osmotic process primarily used in wastewater treatment and purification. It has recently gained recognition as a highly promising membrane technology due to its simplicity, low energy consumption, and minimal fouling compared to pressure-driven membrane technologies. However, the application of the FO process faces challenges such as concentration polarization, reverse solute diffusion, and fouling. Enhancing FO performance requires an efficient membrane that offers high water flux, low solute flux, reduced fouling, and controlled concentration polarization. Among the most effective membranes are polyamide thin-film composite (PA-TFC) membranes, which are created through interfacial polymerization (IP) on porous supports and are mostly used in water treatment and desalination. Currently, there is a need to develop innovative FO membranes that achieve a fine balance between water permeability and solute rejection in the top layer. Additionally, the support layer must be designed to ensure high permeate flow. This paper reviews recent advancements in PA-TFC FO membrane processes and fabrication techniques aimed at modifying the top active layer and support layer, along with other modifications such as interlayered and double-skinned membranes. Finally, further research is required to investigate the scalability of these modification techniques for practical applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Forward osmosis (FO) is an extensively studied osmotic process primarily used in wastewater treatment and purification. It has recently gained recognition as a highly promising membrane technology due to its simplicity, low energy consumption, and minimal fouling compared to pressure-driven membrane technologies. However, the application of the FO process faces challenges such as concentration polarization, reverse solute diffusion, and fouling. Enhancing FO performance requires an efficient membrane that offers high water flux, low solute flux, reduced fouling, and controlled concentration polarization. Among the most effective membranes are polyamide thin-film composite (PA-TFC) membranes, which are created through interfacial polymerization (IP) on porous supports and are mostly used in water treatment and desalination. Currently, there is a need to develop innovative FO membranes that achieve a fine balance between water permeability and solute rejection in the top layer. Additionally, the support layer must be designed to ensure high permeate flow. This paper reviews recent advancements in PA-TFC FO membrane processes and fabrication techniques aimed at modifying the top active layer and support layer, along with other modifications such as interlayered and double-skinned membranes. Finally, further research is required to investigate the scalability of these modification techniques for practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: By 2050, it is anticipated that the global population will surpass 9 billion, which will not only lead to a greater demand for water but also an expected 55 % surge in its consumption. This will escalate competition over water resources, putting an additional 2.3 billion individuals at risk of experiencing extreme scarcity in river basins with severe water stress, as indicated by research from the Organization for Economic and Cooperative Development (OECD) [1]. UN reports also highlight that nearly 2.3 billion individuals globally do not have access to safe drinking water and face substantial shortages, with about 733 million of them suffering from extreme and severe water stress. The growing crisis of water scarcity affects all continents, with underprivileged communities suffering the most. This issue could stem from various factors including usage outstripping available supplies, insufficient water infrastructure, and inadequate management of water distribution to meet the needs of all [2].

Addressing the challenge of limited freshwater sources and the increasing demand for water in households and industries, utilizing methods like seawater and brackish water desalination, along with the recycling of wastewater, emerge as leading solutions to ensure an adequate water supply [3]. In this context, membrane technology stands out as a globally recognized method for both purification and separation, playing a critical role in providing fresh water [4,5]. In particular, the use of membrane technology in water desalination has seen significant advancements recently because it offers efficient separation, is environmentally friendly due to it avoiding creating secondary pollutants, and has superior energy efficiency. This is in contrast to thermal desalination methods that consume more energy and bring higher setup and operational costs due to their dependence on energy [6].

Membrane technologies are categorized based on the type of force applied, such as pressure-driven membranes (including microfiltration (MF), ultrafiltration (UF), nanofiltration (NF), and reverse osmosis (RO)), membranes powered by electricity (like electrodialysis and fuel cells), those that operate on concentration gradients (such as dialysis, pervaporation, and forward osmosis), and membranes that function due to temperature differentials (membrane distillation (MD)) [6]. Among these, RO is notably one of the most extensively utilized membrane technologies. It operates on energy to exert an external force to process fluids [7]. Currently, over 20,000 industrial facilities worldwide have adopted this method [8]. RO is prominent for converting seawater and brackish water into potable water [9] and for extracting harmful contaminants from drinking water [10]. Nonetheless, the energy demand for RO remains substantial even with significant advancements and continuous efforts aimed at reduction over recent decades. In addition to this, severe fouling and concentrated waste streams pose significant obstacles to RO operations [11].

An innovative membrane technique known as forward osmosis (FO) has recently emerged for saltwater desalination and wastewater treatment [12–19]. The principle of FO is derived from the natural osmotic process, which induces water to migrate from an area with a lower solute concentration (feed solution) to one with a higher solute concentration (draw solution), driven by the osmotic pressure differential (∆π) through a semipermeable membrane, thereby moving water from a region of lower osmotic pressure to a region of higher osmotic pressure [20,21], see Fig. 1. This unique characteristic of FO membranes allows them to operate without hydraulic pressure, unlike RO membranes. As a result, FO offers several notable benefits, including lower energy usage, reduced membrane fouling, enhanced rejection of various solutes and contaminants, and simplified membrane cleaning procedures [22–24].

FO technology has attracted considerable attention over the last decade [25,26]. Since the first study on FO by Kessler & Moody [27], which proposed the use of the FO process for desalination, the publication on FO has grown substantially, as shown in Fig. 2. FO systems involve two solutions: a feed solution (FS) and a draw solution (DS). As a result, the FS is the solution to be concentrated or treated, while the DS is the solution with a higher concentration of solutes driving water transport across the FO membrane [28,29] as shown in Fig. 3. The process is divided into two main steps: transferring water from the FS to the DS and the regeneration of the draw solution to recover the water and recycle it. The process begins when water molecules in the FS naturally move across the membrane into a concentrated DS through osmosis, as the draw solution typically has a higher osmotic pressure than the feed solution. To regenerate the draw solution and extract pure water from the system, different techniques can be employed, such as RO [30], MD [31], NF [32], UF [33], heating [34] and heating with solar energy [35].

In the FO process, the membrane is a critical component that must be tailored to precise specifications, ensuring it has a minimal structural thickness [4]. Based on their materials, the two most frequently utilized membrane types in the FO process are cellulose-based membranes and thin film composite (TFC) membranes [36]. Recently, TFC membranes have become the most prevalent choice for FO applications and have gained significant popularity as opposed to conventional asymmetric membranes such as cellulose acetate (CA) and cellulose triacetate (CTA) [36,37], see Fig. 4. Basically, TFC membranes are composed of an ultra-thin selective layer typically made from polyamide (PA), which is supported by a porous layer that is typically made from polymer materials like polysulfone (PSf) and polyethersulfone (PES) fabricated by phase inversion method [38,39]. PSF and PES are often chosen for their excellent chemical properties and satisfactory mechanical durability [40].

Generally, an optimal FO membrane should possess a top active layer characterized by substantial water permeability and minimal reverse solute diffusion (the movement of salts from the draw solution to the feed) and a microporous substrate conducive to high flux [11]. However, in practical scenarios, FO membranes often suffer from significant reverse solute diffusion, intense concentration polarization (CP), and insufficient mechanical durability during FO operations [41,42]. Additionally, even though FO systems are less prone to fouling than pressure-driven processes, fouling remains a critical issue that negatively impacts the efficiency of FO processes [43]. FO processes primarily utilize two types of membrane configurations: one where the active layer is directed towards the feed solution (AL-FS) and one facing the draw solution (AL-DS) [44]. Both arrangements in TFC FO membranes may experience reduced performance due to internal and external concentration polarization (ICP and ECP, respectively) and fouling phenomena.
Though osmotically driven membrane processes have been extensively proposed and examined for various uses, FO continues to face significant obstacles as follows:

Concentration polarization (CP) is a common phenomenon encountered in both pressure-driven and osmosis-driven membrane processes, although it is more pronounced in osmotically-driven processes like FO due to their inherent nature [45,46]. In the FO process, this phenomenon arises from the concentration differences between the FS and DS. CP refers to the concentration of FS on the membrane surface, resulting from water flux through the membrane while simultaneously diluting the DS on the other side [36,46]. This leads to unsatisfactory levels of water flux and reverse salt flux due to significant mass transfer resistance developing both inside and around membranes during osmosis process [47]. Consequently, CP reduces the actual effective osmotic pressure gradient below the osmotic pressure difference between the FS and DS [46].

In essence, CP occurs on the membrane surface, referred to as external concentration polarization (ECP), and within the porous support layer of the membrane, known as internal concentration polarization (ICP). The impact of CP is influenced by the membrane's characteristics and orientation [47]. CP can be either dilutive or concentrative, depending on whether the active layer faces the feed solution (AL-FS) in FO mode or the active layer faces the draw solution (AL-DS) in pressure retarded osmosis (PRO) mode [45], as shown in Fig. 5. The AL-FS orientation (FO mode) leads to concentrative ECP and dilutive ICP occurring concurrently. In contrast, AL-DS orientation (PRO mode) experiences dilutive ECP and concentrative ICP [48,49].

As the FS passes over the membrane's active layer, solutes accumulate on its surface, resulting in concentrative ECP. This phenomenon is comparable to concentration polarization in pressure-driven membrane processes (like RO) [50]. In a composite/asymmetric FO membrane, the salt concentration rises on the feed side and declines on the draw side due to water permeating from the FS to the DS. This ECP may diminish the driving force by reducing the effective concentration gradient throughout the FO membrane's active layer. Enhancing hydrodynamic properties such as increasing crossflow velocity and flow turbulence can help relieve ECP and enhance the effective concentration gradient, thereby improving water flux [22,51].

However, while ECP can reduce the effective concentration gradient in the FO process, numerous studies have shown that ECP plays a minor role in osmotically driven membrane processes and is not the primary cause for the observed decrease in water flux below expected levels. [52,53]. This is because the FO process can be utilized without hydraulic pressure, resulting in a reduced impact of membrane fouling caused by ECP on water flux. However, it is important to note that a study by Suh & Lee [54] suggests that ECP cannot be neglected under low cross flow or when the feed solution has a high solute concentration, such as when desalinating sea water with a high salt content.

Similar to ECP, two types of ICP can occur, such as dilutive ICP and concentrative ICP, depending on membrane orientation. Dilutive ICP occurs in the FO mode due to the dilution of draw solutes by water permeating within the membrane support layer. In contrast, concentrative ICP occurs in the PRO mode as a result of feed solute buildup inside the porous support. In any given membrane orientation, both ICP and ECP occur concurrently [53]. Nevertheless, in most FO processes, it is mainly necessary to consider dilutive ICP when operating in FO mode. Under such circumstances, the impact of ECP might be minimal, particularly when employing pure deionized (DI) water as the feed, maintaining a high feed flow rate, and having an insignificant reverse salt flux. Likewise, in PRO mode, only dilutive ECP should be taken into account, and the effect of concentrative ICP may be considered negligible when using DI water as the feed and maintaining an insignificant reverse salt flux [51].

Besides concentration polarization, membrane fouling is another significant concern that can impact the performance of membrane processes and reduce water flux through the membrane [58]. The FO process is probably less prone to fouling issues, as the driving force compensation is influenced by the varying levels of ICP [59]. Although fouling is less evident in FO membranes compared to other membrane processes, it can still become a severe issue in the long run if membranes are not periodically cleaned [60].

Fouling of the membrane typically takes place when particles or dissolved substances accumulate on the membrane surface or within the pores of the support layer [41]. This accumulation leads to pore blockages, the formation of a cake/gel coating on the membrane surface, and a decrease in membrane permeability [61]. Fouling negatively affects the performance of the membrane in several ways, such as causing concentration polarization, reducing the retention capabilities of the membrane, and adding extra hydraulic resistance [62].

In the FO membrane, fouling occurs in various forms and can be divided into four categories: biofouling, colloidal fouling, organic fouling, and inorganic fouling [42]. Biofouling, also referred to as microbial fouling, is a complicated process involving microorganisms from the feed attaching to the membrane surface and subsequently forming a biofilm, which increases water resistance [62,63]. Meanwhile, colloidal fouling occurs when suspended particles like clay, silica, corrosion products, and bacteria gather on the surface, creating less porous layers and resulting in reduced water flux. Organic fouling, on the other hand, occurs due to the deposition and adsorption of organic compounds typically found in natural organic matter, such as humic substances, proteins, and amino acids [37]. Lastly, inorganic fouling is a result of inorganic precipitate accumulation, such as metal sulfates and carbonates present in water settling near or on the membrane surface and followed by crystallization and crystal growth [64].

Depending on the orientation of the membrane, fouling could be external or internal [61]. In the FO mode (AL-FS mode), foulants accumulate on the active layer, resulting in a cake or gel layer formation. This type of fouling is known as external fouling [65] as described in Fig. 6a. The observation shows that the FO membrane displays a reduced tendency for fouling in AL-FS orientation [65–67] . Despite this, the primary disadvantage of AL-FS orientation is the significantly increased dilutive ICP and decreased initial water flux [66,68]. Surface roughness plays a role in this fouling process, with rougher surfaces exhibiting higher fouling tendencies [69]. Numerous studies have shown that decreased surface roughness prevents the adhesion of accumulation of foulants on the dense active layer, thus making them easier to clean and remove [70].

The issues of external fouling can be addressed using physical and chemical techniques, as well as through surface membrane modification [62]. Physical approaches involve altering the feed water hydrodynamic conditions, such as increasing crossflow velocity [70,71], using a feed spacer [72], introducing pulsed flow [73], and implementing air scouring [74]. However, when dealing with complicated feeds, physical methods alone are insufficient to reduce membrane fouling; thus chemical cleaning becomes necessary [75]. Even though it is less desirable due to its negative impact on membrane lifespan [74], and environmental concerns [71].

In contrast, the fouling mechanism in PRO mode (AL-DS mode) is more complex [61]. When foulant particles are smaller than the support layer pores, fouling occurs through adsorption of these particles onto the pore walls of the support layer or accumulation on the active layer backside, causing pore blockage or internal fouling [65] (Fig. 6b). Pore blockage is the more severe form of fouling and is difficult to remove [76]. Moreover, trapping foulants in the support layer decreases porosity and concentrative ICP impacts, resulting in reduced water flux [69]. Though the osmotic backwash method has been designed to clear foulants from the support layer [77]It is essential to develop a support layer with a high hydrophilicity level and a low structure parameter. This will result in innovative antifouling membranes that can improve the effectiveness of the FO process.

To effectively mitigate fouling within TFC-based FO membranes, early detection and monitoring can be crucial. Several novel fouling detection methods have emerged in recent years, providing more accurate, real-time monitoring capabilities. These methods could include, but not limited, electric impedance methods, dynamic optical tomography (DOTM), microwave sensors, optical sensors, and nanotechnology-based sensors. Each of these approaches offers unique advantages in terms of sensitivity, accuracy, and the ability to detect fouling at different stages.
•
Electric Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)
Electric impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a non-invasive method that measures the electrical impedance of a membrane system to detect fouling. As fouling accumulates on the membrane surface, it alters the electrical properties (e.g., conductivity and capacitance) of the membrane [78]. By monitoring changes in impedance, fouling can be detected in real-time. EIS is highly sensitive to changes in the membrane’s electrical properties, allowing for early detection of fouling, especially biofouling and organic fouling [78,79]. Additionally, it is capable of distinguishing between different types of fouling based on their unique impedance signatures. EIS has been successfully applied in monitoring fouling in TFC-based FO membranes, particularly in scenarios where biofouling and organic fouling are prevalent, such as wastewater treatment and seawater desalination [69,80,81].
•
Dynamic Optical Tomography (DOTM)
Dynamic optical tomography (DOTM) is an advanced imaging technique that uses light scattering to produce real-time images of the fouling layer on the membrane surface [82]. This method utilizes a laser or LED source to project light through the membrane, which is then captured by photodetectors to analyze the scattering patterns [83]. DOTM offers high spatial resolution and can provide a detailed visualization of fouling layer formation and growth over time [82,84]. It is particularly effective for detecting particulate and organic fouling, as well as for assessing fouling dynamics and distribution across the membrane surface [83]. DOTM is suitable for lab-scale and pilot-scale applications where precise monitoring of fouling development is required. It has been applied to study fouling in TFC-based FO membranes used in the treatment of complex industrial effluents [85,86].
•
Microwave sensors
Microwave sensors are another novel method for detecting membrane fouling [87]. These sensors operate by emitting microwave signals that interact with the fouling layer on the membrane surface [88]. Changes in the reflected or transmitted microwave signals are used to detect the presence and extent of fouling. Microwave sensors can penetrate both the membrane and fouling layers, providing a comprehensive analysis of fouling characteristics [87,88]. The sensors are particularly effective for detecting water content and dielectric properties, making them suitable for identifying various types of fouling (e.g., biofouling, scaling, and organic fouling) [89]. Microwave sensors have shown potential in monitoring fouling in TFC-based FO membranes in real-time, especially in applications involving brackish water desalination and the treatment of saline wastewater, where scaling and biofouling are major concerns [89].
•
Optical sensors
Optical sensors utilize light-based techniques, such as fluorescence or absorbance spectroscopy, to detect membrane fouling [90]. These sensors detect changes in the optical properties of the membrane surface, such as colour or fluorescence intensity, which correspond to fouling accumulation [82,90]. Optical sensors provide non-contact, real-time monitoring and are highly sensitive to specific types of fouling, such as organic fouling and biofouling [82]. Such sensors can be easily integrated into existing membrane systems for continuous monitoring. Optical sensors are widely used in monitoring biofouling in TFC-based FO membranes in water treatment applications, including municipal wastewater treatment and seawater desalination plants [91,92].
•
Nanotechnology-based sensors

Nanotechnology-based sensors use nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene oxide, and metal nanoparticles to detect fouling at a molecular level [93–95]. These sensors can identify changes in chemical composition, surface roughness, and other properties associated with fouling [93]. Nanotechnology-based sensors offer ultra-sensitive detection capabilities and can detect fouling at the molecular level before it becomes visible or measurable by conventional methods [94]. Such sensors are particularly effective in detecting early-stage fouling and differentiating between different fouling types. These sensors are suitable for use in advanced FO membrane systems for high-value applications, such as the treatment of pharmaceutical wastewater and the desalination of high-salinity water, where early detection of fouling is critical [59].

In the FO process, one of the primary challenges is reverse solute diffusion (RSD) from the draw solution, which cannot be avoided [42]. A significant concentration difference between the FS and DS causes solute molecules in the draw solution to migrate into the feed solution through the membrane in the opposite direction to the water flux [54], as shown in Fig. 7.

In theory, FO membranes are semipermeable, allowing only solvent molecules to pass through and preventing dissolved solutes from entering the feed solution. However, most FO membranes do not provide perfect solute retention, resulting in RSD. [43,96]. RSD not only decreases the driving force (osmotic pressure difference) across the membrane but also can exacerbate membrane fouling and enhance concentration polarization [37,61,77], therefore reducing the overall efficiency of the FO process. As a result, achieving a high solute rejection is crucial since it is one of the key aspects in determining the performance of FO membranes.

In this regard, it is worth noting that the selection of a draw solution is essential to minimize the RSD. It is crucial to select a draw solution that establishes an effective osmotic gradient while simultaneously reducing RSD. The migration of solute from the DS to the FS referred to as reverse solute flux (RSF or Js), not only results in draw solution loss and feed solution contamination but also raises the replacement cost of the draw solution [51]. Hence, maintaining a minimal reverse flow of the draw solute is critical for a successful FO process.

Nevertheless, it has been suggested that RSD phenomena could be used advantageously to enhance the FO process. A review study conducted by Wu et al. [97] discussed how RSD, generally seen as a disadvantage, can be used to improve filtration efficiency, facilitate resource recovery, and explore new applications. The primary claims about FO membrane performance involve adjusting the chemical properties of feed solutions, like pH and salt concentration, through RSD to enhance membrane antifouling properties and increase selectivity [97].

Efficient separation of water from the draw solution is critical for maintaining the quality of the product water in FO processes. Inefficient separation can lead to contamination of the treated water and reduce the effectiveness of the FO process [98,99]. Furthermore, this leads to lower water recovery rates and higher energy consumption for subsequent treatment steps, such as RO or MD, used to separate the draw solute from the water [100]. This reduces the overall efficiency and cost-effectiveness of the FO process. These challenges have prompted researchers to investigate advanced separation methods and novel draw solutes to enhance the efficiency of FO processes [101]. One approach to improving separation efficiency is the development of hybrid systems that combine FO with other membrane technologies. Researchers are exploring advanced separation methods, such as hybrid systems that combine FO with other membrane technologies (e.g., FO-RO, FO-MD), to enhance separation efficiency [101,102]. Magnetic nanoparticle-assisted separation and the use of responsive draw solutes that facilitate easier separation are also promising approaches to tackle these challenges [98,103].

Draw solution regeneration is vital for maintaining the driving force in TFC-based FO processes. Over time, the draw solution becomes diluted as water permeates from the feed solution, leading to a decrease in osmotic pressure differential, which is the key driver of water flux in FO systems [36]. Inefficient regeneration can result in diminished water flux, reduced system efficiency, and increased operational costs. Particularly, Inadequate draw solution regeneration directly reduces the osmotic pressure gradient, leading to lower water flux and reduced overall process efficiency. For instance, studies have shown that certain draw solutions, such as ammonium bicarbonate, can be thermally regenerated; however, the energy requirements for such processes can be substantial, negating the low-energy benefits of FO [20,36,98–100].

To mitigate these issues, research is focusing on low-energy regeneration techniques such as using renewable energy sources (solar or geothermal energy) or developing advanced draw solutions that are easier to regenerate [29,37]. Electrochemical regeneration methods, which utilize electric fields to facilitate solute recovery, are also being explored to enhance energy efficiency and sustainability [29,101]. For example, electrochemical regeneration methods, which utilize electric fields to facilitate solute recovery, are also being explored to enhance energy efficiency and sustainability [104].

Low water flux is a critical challenge in FO processes, particularly when using TFC membranes. Factors such as ICP, membrane fouling, and suboptimal draw solution properties contribute to reduced flux rates [98,99]. Low flux rates lead to reduced water recovery efficiency and prolonged operational times, impacting the economic viability of FO systems for water treatment. Additionally, low flux can exacerbate membrane fouling, as foulants tend to accumulate more rapidly on membranes with low permeate flow [85,100]. Enhancements in membrane material properties, by incorporating nanomaterials (e.g., graphene oxide, TiO2) to reduce ICP, improve hydrophilicity and antifouling properties have shown potential to increase flux rates [102,105–108]. Moreover, optimizing draw solution concentration, temperature, and flow dynamics can help achieve higher flux rates [98,105].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scaling-up from laboratory to commercial scale
ce:section.ce:para: Scaling up TFC-based FO membranes from laboratory or pilot-scale to commercial-scale applications poses significant challenges, including maintaining consistent membrane performance, managing fouling and CP, and optimizing draw solution management in larger systems [98,106]. At the commercial scale, the uniformity of membrane properties (such as thickness, surface charge, and hydrophilicity) can vary, leading to inconsistent water flux and salt rejection across different membrane modules [98]. For instance, a study by Tiraferri et al. [109] demonstrated that variations in the substrate structure of TFC membranes can lead to substantial differences in water flux and solute rejection, with water fluxes ranging from 4 to 25 LMH under similar operating conditions. Additionally, issues such as increased fouling rates and CP can reduce membrane efficiency and lifespan when exposed to large volumes of feed water with varying qualities.

Advanced manufacturing techniques, such as roll-to-roll fabrication and precision coating, are being developed to ensure uniform membrane quality at scale [107,108]. Furthermore, robust process control systems, including real-time monitoring and adaptive cleaning protocols, are crucial for maintaining membrane performance in large-scale operations [110]. While scaling up TFC-based FO membranes presents significant challenges, ongoing research in membrane fabrication techniques, fouling mitigation strategies, and process optimization offers promising solutions for commercial-scale applications.

The high cost of TFC membrane materials presents a significant barrier to the widespread adoption of FO technology. Costs are driven by factors such as the use of expensive polymers, the need for specialized membrane fabrication nanomaterials, and the manufacturing processes involved in producing high-quality membranes [29,98,99]. The high cost of materials affects the economic feasibility of FO systems, particularly in large-scale applications where membrane replacement can be frequent and costly. High material costs may also limit access to advanced FO technology in regions with limited financial resources.

Traditional TFC-FO membranes often use costly polymers such as polyamide for the active layer [98]. For example, m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC), commonly used in interfacial polymerization to form the polyamide layer, are relatively expensive chemicals [99]. Moreover, many high-performance TFC-FO membranes incorporate nanomaterials to enhance their properties. For instance, carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, and metal-organic frameworks are used to improve water flux and selectivity. However, these materials can significantly increase production costs [20]. Besides, the fabrication of TFC-FO membranes often involves multi-step processes, including the preparation of a porous support layer and the formation of an ultrathin selective layer through interfacial polymerization. These intricate processes require specialized equipment, contributing to higher costs [111].

To reduce costs, research is focused on developing low-cost, high-performance membrane materials, such as cellulose-based or alternative polymers that offer comparable performance to traditional polyamide membranes [29]. Researchers are developing simplified manufacturing processes to reduce production costs. For example, a single-step fabrication method for TFC-FO membranes has been proposed, potentially lowering manufacturing expenses [29,98].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of flat sheet polyamide TFC FO membrane
Synthesis of support layer by phase inversion method
ce:section.ce:para: The phase inversion method is the most employed technique for fabricating flat sheet microporous support or substrate membranes [40]. It involves controlling the conversion of a homogeneous polymer solution from a liquid to a solid state. Typically, the homogeneous liquid undergoes a transition into two separate liquids (referred to as liquid-liquid demixing) before the polymer solidifies [112].

There are four different techniques for the phase inversion process, including nonsolvent-induced phase separation (NIPS), thermally induced phase separation (TIPS), vapor-induced phase separation (VIPS), and evaporation-induced phase separation (EIPS) [112]. Out of these methods, nonsolvent-induced phase separation (NIPS) remains the most prevalent method for creating flat sheet microporous membranes. The primary reason for this is the simplicity of preparing desirable hydrophilic substrates, which can be obtained through this method [39,79].

In the non-solvent induced phase inversion (NIPS) technique, a uniform polymer solution is created using an appropriate solvent (dope solution). Firstly, the polymer solution will be poured on a flat, clean, and dry glass surface, followed by casting using an adjustable doctor blade. The polymer solution, along with the glass plate, is subsequently submerged in a coagulation bath containing a non-solvent (usually water), which causes the exchange of solvent and non-solvent to form the polymer membrane [79–81], as shown in Fig. 8.

The NIPS method offers great flexibility in adjusting membrane pore size and morphology [113]. Various factors can impact the final membrane structure created through NIPS. These involve the primary membrane-forming material's features, such as the polymer solution concentration, additives (nanofillers), and solvent type, as well as synthesis conditions like the coagulation bath, polymer solution temperature, evaporation temperature, humidity, and post-processing (drying technique and duration) [40,45,82]. The polymer solvent is crucial in determining the ultimate membrane characteristics. An effective solvent result in a uniform polymer distribution, leading to fewer deficiencies in the membrane structure, such as pinholes or fractures [114]. Changing these factors can have a significant impact not only on membrane substrate characteristics such as pore size, porosity, thickness, and hydrophilicity but also on its interaction with the polyamide layer during interfacial polymerization (IP). This results in the formation of composite membranes exhibiting a wide range of properties [20,79].

In the fabrication of support layers (substrates), PSF and PES are the predominant utilized polymers for the TFC membranes [115], [116]. This is attributed to the numerous benefits offered by PSF and PES, including thermal and chemical resistance, hydrolytic and mechanical stability, high structural polarity, and extensive pH tolerance [40,79,86]. Nonetheless, these polymers also possess certain drawbacks, such as relatively hydrophobicity and comparatively higher costs when compared to cellulose acetate. Consequently, it is often necessary to modify PSF and PES membranes with hydrophilic materials in order to enhance their wettability, fouling resistance, and permeability [87–89].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of polyamide active layer by interfacial polymerization
ce:section.ce:para: Following the synthesis of the support layer, the TFC FO membrane is prepared by forming a polyamide (PA) thin active, selective rejection layer on top of the porous support membrane. The active layers of most TFC FO membranes are produced through interfacial polymerization (IP) at a liquid-liquid interface, involving the polycondensation reaction between two primary active monomers [40,85,90,91]. A range of nucleophilic reactants, generally multifunctional amines, and electrophilic reactants, usually multifunctional acid chlorides, are frequently employed as monomers in IP [92,93].

Mainly, MPD and TMC monomers are the most popular for forming the PA active layer in TFC membranes [11,92,94]. Both monomers have a benzene ring, leading to these membranes being classified as fully aromatic PA membranes [117]. These monomers have indeed played a significant role in supplying aromatic crosslinked PA layers with high water fluxes and high salt rejections [95,118].

Typically, the IP process begins by immersing the support or substrate in an aqueous solution filled with an amine-rich monomer (MPD). After the excess MPD on the surface has been removed, the amine-soaked membrane is placed into an organic solvent containing acyl chloride monomers (TMC). The interaction between these two monomers at the boundary of the two non-mixing solvents leads to the creation of a thin and dense layer on the substrate's surface [90,97], as illustrated in Fig. 9. Ideally, the resulting polyamide layer should demonstrate both exceptional water permeability and high permselectivity [40].

Numerous factors influence the formation of the active layer through IP, such as the concentration of monomers, the duration of the IP reaction, the temperature of the organic solution, and additives/nanomaterials in either aqueous or organic solutions [93,97–103]. In addition, characteristics of the support layer such as surface porosity, pore dimensions, and hydrophilicity can directly impact the reaction interface, aqueous monomer enrichment, monomer dispersion uniformity, and monomer diffusion to the interface [120]. These aspects significantly contribute to determining attributes like pore size, thickness, roughness, hydrophilicity, morphology, and cross-linking of the PA thin layer. As a result, these factors ultimately influence the overall performance of the TFC FO membranes [20,45,84].

The PA active layer in TFC FO membranes plays a crucial role in separation performance. Modifying the surface properties of the active layer aims to improve the performance of separation and purification processes and address challenges like surface fouling and the trade-off between water permeability and solute selectivity. [121]. The characteristics of membrane separation and fouling are often linked to the properties of the PA surface [3]. The key attributes of the PA surface that matter most are hydrophilicity, cross-linking degree, surface roughness, and surface charge [5,122]. Researchers have been exploring various surface modification approaches to enhance the PA TFC membrane characteristics. The following subsections will present a review of the typically used techniques for modifying active layers, including layer-by-layer assembly, blending nanomaterials, surface grafting, and doping additives into IP process. Some of these techniques can be implemented during IP process, while others may be applied after the formation of the PA layer.

The layer-by-layer assembly (LbL) method offers an alternative approach to coating support layers and has recently gained increased attention as a relatively novel technique. [45]. The LbL polyelectrolytes (PE) self-assembly is commonly utilized in constructing high-performance FO membranes with varying structures to ensure sufficient water flow and adjustable solute rejection [123]. The flexibility of this process lies in the ability to control the number of added layers and the option to use different types of PE [124]. Moreover, it can be employed to develop an ultra-thin, free defect layer with excellent thermal stability and high solvent resistance, thereby improving membrane performance [106–108].

In the LbL assembly process, layers of PE with opposite charges are successively deposited onto a charged substrate layer to create a thin, selective film that is linked through electrostatic absorption and van der Waals force. [45,105,125]. This technique involves building multilayers of PE by consecutively adhering polyions (polycations and polyanions) onto a porous substrate layer, either physically or mechanically, through methods such as spraying, dip-coating, or spin-coating [126].

Although the advantages of this approach are numerous, it has certain drawbacks, including a poor rejection of very small ion draw solutions (NaCl) and a limited choice of draw solutions when employing TFC FO membranes. Some research has managed to attain NaCl rejection rates higher than 85 % utilizing LbL-based membranes [109,127]. Moreover, it has been noted that in TFC RO LbL-based membranes for desalination, the NaCl rejection is relatively low (<90 %) at low pressures [128]. Kwon et al. [129] conducted a study utilizing an LbL-based membrane to reject NaCl. They employed a molecular layer-by-layer (mLbL) technique to create high-performance TFC FO membranes that exhibited exceptional rejection of monovalent NaCl salt. They formed an ultrathin, dense PA selective layer on a hydrolyzed polyacrylonitrile (HPAN) support layer coated with a bilayer of polyethyleneimine (PEI) and polyacrylic acid (PAA) polyelectrolytes using mLbL [129], (see Fig. 10). By adjusting the number of mLbL cycles (1–12 cycles), they fine-tuned the separation properties of these membranes. The highest performance was achieved at 10 mLbL cycles, resulting in a solute rejection (R%) of about 92.4 %, determined through an RO filtration system at an operating pressure of 5 bar. The optimal mLbL-10 membrane displayed superior performance compared to commercial HTI and hand-cast TFC membranes. With 0.5 M NaCl as a draw solution, the optimal membrane in FO mode had a water flux of approximately 24.5 LMH and an RSF of around 2.36 gMH. In PRO mode, the water flux reached 34.6 LMH with an RSF of about 3.24 gMH [129].

Recently, Rafiee et al. [15] synthesized a PA TFO FO membrane featuring a triple layer on the surface of the PES support layer, using the LbL assembly technique for removing heavy metal ions from wastewater. They employed a hydrophilic alginate/Ca2 + thin layer as an interlayer between PA and PES via the IP process to enhance membrane selectivity and permeability. The impact of the interlayer and LbL cycles (1–7 cycles) on the membrane's morphology and performance were examined, showing thinner and smoother PA layers when an interlayer was used [15]. The modified membrane demonstrated increased water flux, decreased reverse salt flux, and higher heavy metal ion rejection compared to the control TFC membrane. The optimal results were observed for the modified TFC-LbL.5 membrane, which had twice the water flux at about 23.3 LMH and an RSF of approximately 0.6 gMH using a 1 M NaCl draw solution. Furthermore, under FO mode, heavy metal ion rejection (Cr3 +, Co2 +, and Hg2 +) exceeded 99 % [15]. Zhang et al. [130] also developed a PA TFC FO membrane using the LbL technique to improve chlorine resistance and water permeability. The PA layer was created through the self-polymerization process using 3,5-Dihydroxybenzoic acid (DHBA) and TMC, along with the addition of 4-amino-benzene sulfonamide (4-ABSA), a sulfonamide monomer, on a PSF support layer. Three different membrane types (DHBA, DHBA-ABSA, and ABSA-TMC) underwent performance testing, with the DHBA-ABSA membrane demonstrating a superior water flux and a reduced RSF of 22 LMH and 2.94 gMH, respectively, using 1 M NaCl as draw solution. In comparison to the MPD-TMC membrane, the water flux of the three modified membranes—DHBA, ABSA, and DHBA combined with ABSA— increased by 27.6 %, 44.0 %, and 67.6 %, respectively, while the reverse salt flux experienced a reduction of 9.9 %, 12.3 %, and 16.2 % respectively [130].

Besides polyelectrolytes, nanomaterials can also be utilized in the LbL method. In the research conducted by Salehi et al. [131] they applied LbL assembly of positively charged chitosan (CS) and negatively charged graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets through electrostatic interaction on a sulfonated polyethersulfone polyethersulfone (SPES-PES) support layer to create PA TFC FO membrane, as depicted in Fig. 11. CS was chosen to crosslink GO nanosheets on the negatively charged SPES-PES substrate, improving interfacial compatibility. LbL assembly significantly enhanced FO membrane performance. LbL membranes had higher water flux values with less salt permeability than TFC membranes. Additionally, a lower fouling propensity was observed in LbL membranes when exposed to sodium alginate (SA) foulant [131]. Moreover, Kang et al. [132] they employed two nanomaterials, GO and oxidized carbon nanotubes (OCNTs), which were LbL assembled on a PES support layer and combined with poly dimethyl diallyl ammonium chloride (PDDA) to improve the stability and dispersibility of the nanomaterials. Two distinct membranes were developed: GO-OCNTs-LbL and GO-LbL. The findings demonstrated that the GO-OCNTs-LbL membrane had superior NaCl rejection performance compared to the GO-LbL membrane, exhibiting a 70.2 % reduction in solute flux. This improvement can be attributed to the presence of OCNTs, which limited the expansion of GO within the membrane structure [132].

In summary, employing the LbL technique offers significant benefits, including a boost in water flux, superior rejection of heavy metal ions rejection in wastewater purification, improved resistance to fouling resistance, and strengthened membrane durability. Nonetheless, improvements are needed to utilize this method for water desalination and NaCl rejection. Furthermore, LbL-based membranes involve additional steps and materials compared to traditional TFC FO membranes, potentially raising the cost and complicating the fabrication process.

Incorporating various nanomaterials into the PA active layer to create thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes with high water permeability and low RSD is currently the most popular approach for modifying TFC membranes [4,103,133]. Generally, these nanomaterials enhance hydrophilicity, porosity, and surface charge, as well as create channels for water molecule transport within the membrane. Although this results in increased water flux, it is usually at the expense of salt rejection. This is due to possible voids forming between nanomaterials and the PA matrix [134].

Based on characteristics such as polarity, dispersibility, and hydrophilic/hydrophobic properties, nanomaterials can be introduced into either the aqueous or organic phase and then incorporated into the PA active layer during the IP process (see Fig. 12) [103,133]. Nanomaterials like zeolites, silica, Ag, TiO2, GO, and CNTs that have previously been employed in RO nanocomposite membranes have also been investigated for the creation of TFN FO membranes [86,135].

Moreover, the shape and dimension of nanomaterials are crucial in the construction of TFN membranes, as they significantly impact their efficiency. [137]. Nanomaterials can be classified into non-porous (solid nanoparticles), porous nanomaterials, and one- dimensional (1D) and two-dimensional (2D) types. Porous nanomaterials like zeolite [138], silica, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [139], covalent frameworks (COFs) [140] etc., and non-porous nanomaterials such as TiO2 [141], fullerene impregnated porous aromatic framework (C60 @PAF900) [142], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [143] etc., 1D nanomaterials (nanotubes) like carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [144], halloysite nanotubes (HNTs) [145], titanate nanotubes (TNTs) [146] and 2D nanomaterials such as GO [106], MoS2 [147], graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) [148], etc. Nevertheless, the manufacturing conditions of these nanomaterials can result in them being porous or nonporous, and their dimensions may vary as 1D or 2D based on size and shape. In general, incorporating these variously shaped nanomaterials into the PA active layer results in additional transport channels through the formation of nanovoids at the interface between the nanomaterials and the polymer matrix, which affects the solvent and solute transport pathways. [149].

Porous nanomaterials offer additional water transportation pathways through their inherent pore structures [150], also providing solute trapping sites via the sieving effect and electrostatic interactions [18,151]. In contrast, nonporous nanomaterials can enhance the density and make up for active layer imperfections, potentially leading to improved salt rejection in TFN membranes [152]. 2D nanomaterials are comparatively more efficient in increasing the water permeability and selectivity of TFN membranes, owing to their high aspect ratio, extensive specific surface area, and excellent compatibility with the PA matrix [137]. Although 1D nanomaterials with a hollow tube structure also offer extra channels for water molecule transportation without significantly impacting solute rejection [119,153], they may result in defects in the water pathway if the orientation of 1D nanotubes is not precisely aligned with water flow direction across the membrane [154].

Table 1 presents a summary of PA TFN membrane fabrication and its FO performance in comparison to PA TFC membranes. As evident from the table, the water flux of TFN membranes surpasses that of TFC membranes. However, this enhanced water flux may occasionally be accompanied by a minor increase in RSF. For instant Akther et al. [140] integrated hydrophilic Schiff base network-1 (SNW-1) nanoparticles, which are covalent organic frameworks (COFs), into the PA active layer. The optimum loading of SNW-1 was discovered to be 0.005 wt%, resulting in an increase of water flux from 9.3 LMH to 12 LMH and a rise in RSF from 2.88 gMH to 3.48 gMH for TFC and TFN, respectively. These tests were conducted in FO mode using DI water as the FS and 0.5 M NaCl as the DS. The increase in water flux was due to the porous structure of SNW-1, which created more pathways for water molecules to move and reduced the resistance to water transport in the PA layer [140].

Lately, the utilization of hybrid and modified nanomaterials has garnered increased interest for instant Seyedpour et al. [161] synthesized a combination of two different types of nanomaterials consisting of inorganic metal silver (Ag) nanoparticles and metal-organic framework (MOFs), resulting in a nanorod structure. These Ag-MOF nanorods demonstrated high hydrophilicity, negative surface charge, and extended biocidal activity. In another study, Yassari et al. [164] modified GO by functionalizing it with natural tannic acid (TA) macromolecules. The GO-TA bonded with the PA layer through hydrogen and covalent bonds, which enhanced the stability and selectivity of the TFN FO membrane. Similarly, Lee et al. [144] functionalized carbon nanotubes (CNT) with tert-butylamine (TBA) incorporated into the PA layer. This functionalization prevented CNT self-bundling through steric hindrance and surface charge repulsion. Consequently, the enhanced TFN membrane exhibited uniform nanofiller dispersion on the membrane surface and cross-section, resulting in a smooth and defect-free PA layer.

More recently, Bagherzadeh et al. [168] synthesized a unique heterostructure nanomaterial composed of layered double hydroxides (LDHs) containing copper and aluminum ions (CuAl) in combination with tungsten disulfide (WS2) nanosheets. This combination aimed to enhance the desalination and antifouling performance of TFN FO membrane. They found that WS2 nanosheets overlapped each other and with LDH nanosheets created vacant spaces, that act as nanochannels for water transport and separation. Moreover, they observed that incorporating WS2/CuAl LDH nanosheets reduced the membrane surface roughness and the presence of ridges and valleys in the PA layer, thus enhancing the membrane antifouling properties.

Overall, integrating nanomaterials into the PA layer can enhance water permeability by generating nanochannels and improve salt rejection by sealing pores or creating smaller nanochannels in the PA layer. Additionally, it can enhance the hydrophilicity of the membrane and lessen its fouling propensity by enhancing surface smoothness, surface charge, and increasing antimicrobial properties of the PA layer. Nevertheless, there are certain challenges that may arise when incorporating nanomaterials into the PA layer. One primary concern is the clustering of these materials, leading to the formation of large nanoparticle aggregates which can diminish their effective surface area and functionality, as well as introduce defects and gaps in the PA layer. Although hybrid and modified nanomaterials offer potential solutions to the agglomeration problem, a balance between permeability and selectivity is still a common issue. As such, determining the optimal design and choice of nanomaterials for specific applications remains a challenge and calls for more research. Finally, the durability and reliability of TFN FO membranes, and monitoring their long-term performance under practical application conditions, is another significant concern that requires attention.

Surface grafting, also known as surface chemical grafting, is a method of modifying the surface chemistry of TFC membranes. This method involves attaching polymer chains or molecules to the PA layer through various chemical reactions, forming covalent bonds that ensure strong interactions between the polymer chains and the membrane selective layer [3,169]. Recently, surface grafting techniques have gained popularity due to their versatility in modifying desired surface attributes [170]. This chemical modification approach demonstrates a significant enhancement in FO membrane performance, comparable to physical modifications such as LbL assembly, blending nanomaterials, and surface coating [43].

This modification technique introduces new functionalities and characteristics to the membrane surface, enhancing its separation performance as well as improving antifouling and chlorine resistance capabilities by grafting hydrophilic materials [129,130]. Specific functional molecules or chains can be accurately grafted to the desired location on the membrane by activating the surface through chemically initiated, plasma-initiated, or irradiation-initiated methods [171]. Common materials utilized in surface grafting include amino acids, acrylic acid, biocidal polymers, and anti-biofouling zwitterionic polymers [3]. The resulting layers of varied topologies in chemical grafting can be generated by varying reactant types, functional group numbers, or modification techniques [172].

Generally, two methods are used to attach functional groups or polymer chains, known as "grafting to" and "grafting from," [173] as shown in Fig. 13. In the grafting-to method, the monomer chain is polymerized before being attached to the membrane surface [3]. This process often entails chemical grafting or condensation reactions between the terminal functional groups, polymer chains, and compatible reactive sites scattered across the membrane surface to create polymer brushes [174]. Notably, no monomer polymerization occurs during polymer brush formation. Though this technique allows for the quick and simple creation of polymer brushes, achieving high-density polymer brushes can be challenging due to the steric effect. This effect happens when previously grafted polymer chains obstruct the attachment of subsequent chains to the surface, limiting the thickness and uniformity of the resulting polymer brushes [173]. More importantly, this grafting-to approach leads to the formation of homopolymers, which can increase grafting rates and consequently enhance the polydispersity of the attached polymer layers [175].

In the "grafting-from" or surface-initiated polymerization process, the membrane surface is initially activated with initiators and subsequently, monomers undergo polymerization. Then, the polymer chain develops from the initiator locations on the membrane surface through propagation reactions [3,137,138]. During grafting, monomers are incorporated into the surface initiation sites, allowing for the expansion of a high-density polymer layer [176]. By adding the monomers over time, the grafting-form method can help control the thickness of the grafting layer; however, achieving a precise final chain length for the attached polymer chains is quite challenging [177]. This technique may also result in homopolymer chain formation (grafting-to) in the solution due to thermal monomer initiation, particularly at elevated monomer concentrations and extended reaction durations. As a result, it is crucial to regulate grafting parameters such as monomer concentration, grafting temperature, and reaction time to prevent excessive homopolymer chain formation that could reduce monomer availability for grafting-from and increase the polydispersity of attached polymer chains [175].

The grafting-from technique has emerged as the preferred method for creating high-density polymer brushes, as it addresses the constraints of the grafting-to approach [173]. This technique is typically carried out using atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP), a controlled radical polymerization (CRP) process that enables accurate control over the molecular weight composition, and structure of polymers [178]. ATRP offers benefits such as controllable chain growth by stopping the polymerization process at a specific duration, moderate polymerization conditions, and a minimal presence of free homopolymer impurities (low dispersity) [177]. Although ATRP has demonstrated promising results in enhancing membrane properties, the reaction environment associated with ATRP significantly impacts membrane selectivity due to its harsh conditions [179]. Redox-initiated grafting can be employed to preserve the permselectivity of the membrane. The primary benefit of this approach is its ability to proceed in aqueous media at room temperature without harming the transport properties of the membranes [180], [181], [182], ultimately making it a more environmentally friendly approach [179]. Through redox-initiated grafting, the membrane surface is modified by attaching polymer chains via a redox reaction. The polymerization process occurs through an interaction between an oxidizing agent and a reducing agent, which can generate free radicals that initiate monomer polymerization [183]. Redox pairs, such as potassium/sodium persulfate and potassium/sodium metabisulfite, are commonly employed due to their effectiveness at room temperature and reasonable radical generation rate at moderate temperatures [184], [185].

Table 2 presents a summary of various research studies conducted in the past five years on surface grafting PA TFC FO membranes. In 2018 Ni & Ge [186] introduced an innovative technique for creating TFC FO membranes by functionalizing the PA layer using aniline sulfonate/bisulfonate compounds through a single step amidation reaction to enhances both water flux and antifouling performance (see Fig. 14). The modification compounds are bifunctional, featuring primary amine and sulfonic acid/sulfonate groups, such as sulfanilic acid (SA), sodium sulfanilate (SA-Na), 2,5-disulfoaniline disodium salt (DSA-2Na), and 2,5-disulfoaniline monosodium salt (DSA-Na) [186]. These substances covalently attach to the PA layer through an amidation reaction between their amine groups and the acyl chloride groups on the PA surface. This reaction is uncomplicated and rapid, requiring no catalysts or initiators. The altered membranes possess a more hydrophilic and smoother surface than unmodified membranes, improving water transport rates and fouling resistance in FO processes. Among the altered membranes, the DSA-2Na modified membrane demonstrates superior performance due to a higher number of ionic sulfonate groups compared to other modification materials [186].

Shen et al. [189] altered the PA TFC FO by chemically modifying its surface using phosphonic acids to enhance separation performance, fouling resistance, and chemical stability for FO applications. The researchers utilized a three-step surface modification technique that encompassed PEI grafting, organic phosphonic or carboxylic acid functionalization, and calcium mineralization. A schematic representation of these sequential modifications can be seen in Fig. 15 [189]. Firstly, the membrane surface undergoes crosslinking with polyethyleneimine (PEI) by reacting the residual acyl chloride groups of the PA layer with PEI amine groups. This process introduces hydrophilic and positively charged amine groups onto the surface while reducing the number of carboxylic acid groups that can attract foulants. Subsequently, the membrane surface is further modified by attaching organic phosphonic or carboxylic acids to PEI amine groups. This step adds more hydrophilic and negatively charged acid groups to the surface, increasing water flux, and diminishing RSF. An exploration of various organic acids with differing quantities of phosphonic or carboxylic acid groups in their molecular structures was conducted to analyze their effects [189]. Lastly, calcium ions are applied to treat the membrane surface, which then chelates with oxygen and nitrogen atoms from the acid groups. This reaction generates a mineralized exterior that enhances membrane hydrophilicity, fouling resistance, and chemical stability. Researchers also compared the results of different calcium ion amounts chelating with various acid groups. The optimal membrane was determined to be one modified by organic phosphonic acid and mineralized with calcium ions. This altered membrane demonstrated high water flux and low RSF coupled with strong antifouling properties and considerable chemical stability [189].

In their subsequent research in 2019, Shen et al. [193] utilized surface grafting of the PA layer with PEI and N-containing organic phosphonic acid (OPA) to create numerous and stable complexing sites for various metal ion solutions (Ag+, Ca2 +, Cu2 +, Fe3 +). Following this, diethylenetriaminepentakis (methylphosphonic acid) (DTPMP) is attached to the PEI-altered layer, introducing stable chelation sites for metal ions. Subsequently, different metal ion solutions are employed to ionize the DTPMP-attached layer, while sodium borohydride (NaBH4) was used to reduce metal ions and form nanoparticles on the membrane surface. [193]. This leads to improved hydrophilicity, antifouling, antibacterial, and separation performance. The most effective membrane was PA-PEI-DTPMP-Ag due to its superior water flux, minimal RSF, exceptional antifouling and antibacterial properties, and stable silver loading. This membrane surpasses the control membrane in FO tests and demonstrates robust anti-adhesive and antibacterial capabilities, making it an ideal option for a variety of applications [193].

Surface grafting can also be employed to incorporate nanomaterials onto membrane surfaces, enhancing the stability and immobility of hybrid nanocomposite membranes. Conventional techniques such as blending, and deposition might result in weak binding forces between the nanomaterials and the polymeric membrane. Surface grafting can address this problem by forming chemical bonds between the nanomaterials and the membrane surface [200]. Seyedpour et al. [192] altered the surface of a PA TFC FO membrane by attaching Ag-MOF nanomaterial, which improved its antibacterial properties. The Ag-MOF formed a covalent bond with unreacted acyl chloride groups in the PA layer. This Ag-MOF-functionalized TFC membrane showed exceptional antibacterial and anti-biofouling properties because the Ag-MOF serves as a repository for silver ions that can be gradually released as positively charged ions, providing long-lasting antibacterial activity, indicating the potential for extended durability and stability [192]. Although the unmodified membrane exhibited a lower water flux, its RSF was reduced when compared to that of unmodified membrane. This occurs due to the transport barrier function of the Ag-MOF, which reduces the membrane surface available for water molecules to pass through. Additionally, the slow release of positively charged silver ions from Ag-MOF changes the charge distribution and affects how the membrane surface interacts with the feed solution interface [192].

Furthermore, zwitterionic materials can be utilized to functionalize the TFC FO membrane. In the most recent study by Chen et al. [199] they modified the PA TFC FO membrane by attaching arginine (Arg), a zwitterionic amino acid, onto the PA layer through an eco-friendly and straightforward amidation reaction. This modification aimed to improve the membrane antifouling and antibacterial properties for oily wastewater treatment. The modified membrane exhibited increased hydrophilicity, a positive surface charge, and superior water flux and salt rejection compared to the control membrane. Additionally, the modified membrane demonstrated outstanding antifouling performance against three kinds of oil in water emulsions stabilized by various surfactants and displayed high antibacterial efficacy against E. coli [199].

In light of the above, it becomes evident that surface grafting of PA TFC FO membrane is an efficient method for altering membrane attributes such as hydrophilicity, fouling resistance, and selectivity. This approach enhances water flux, salt rejection, antifouling, and antibacterial performance, as well as chemical stability. It could be easily applicable to modify a TFC membrane's properties for specific applications. Nevertheless, it is crucial to thoroughly optimize the grafting parameters of the PA surface in the TFC FO membrane. Aspects to take into account are monomer concentration and type, reaction temperature, and duration. Fine-tuning is required to attain the desired qualities and functionality of the modified membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Other additives in the IP process: enhancing PA layer formation
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to incorporating nanofiller additives into the PA layer, various other substances such as surfactants, phase-transfer catalysts, and co-solvents have been introduced into the amine aqueous solution during the IP process to modify the physicochemical and structural properties of the resulting membranes [201]. The addition of these substances during PA layer synthesis is a common strategy to achieve the desired selectivity and permeability in TFC membranes [116]. Although these additives do not become part of the resulting PA polymer [202], they can influence the reaction kinetics and the diffusion rate of the aqueous reactant into the organic phase. Consequently, this modifies the structure, crosslinking degree of the PA layer, and ultimately its separation performance [203].

In a conventional IP process, the reaction mainly occurs on the organic side (TMC solution) of the interface due to the high diffusivity of diamine monomers (MPD) from the aqueous phase into the organic phase [202]. It is widely accepted that this reaction is primarily governed by the diffusion of amine molecules into the organic phase. Therefore, any change in miscibility at the water-organic interface is likely to impact both diffusion and partitioning of amine molecules within the reaction zone, thereby altering the polymerization rate [204]. Introducing these additives into the aqueous solution is believed to modify how amine molecules partition and diffuse into the reaction zone by altering properties at the water-organic interface [201]. This assists in the better interaction of amine solutions with the support layer or aids in transferring amines to the interface during the IP process [205].

It should be noted that the role of these additives in the IP process has not been extensively explored in the FO process. Although more research has been conducted on these additives for RO, NF, and UF processes, this review will focus on their application in the FO process. In 2013, Klaysom et al. [206] optimized the IP process for PA TFC FO membranes based on a PAN substrate by using two different additives: triethylamine (TEA) and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS). TEA was employed to enhance the cross-linking and film formation of the PA layer. However, they discovered that adding TEA to the amine solution resulted in poor salt rejection, which they attributed to disparities in partitioning and diffusivity between TEA and MPD, causing a non-uniform film. Additionally, the pH of the amine solution increased from 9.2 to 12.6 due to TEA, which they suggested could also contribute to poor results as at a PH above 10, acyl chloride hydrolysis is accelerated, leading to reduced cross-linking and lower salt rejection [206]. SDS was used as a surfactant to improve the wettability of the substrate during IP, leading to a uniform coating of the polyamide layer. They suggested that SDS, being negatively charged, enhances the migration of monomers to the interface region and thus improves polymerization efficiency. By adding a small amount of SDS (0.1 wt%), salt retention improved from 56.8 % to 95.6 % without compromising water permeability. However, higher concentrations of SDS led to surface defects due to an excess number of surfactant micelles creating free volume in the PA film, resulting in defects in the selective layer once the surfactant was removed after reaction completion [206]. The researchers also experimented with combining TEA and SDS during the IP reaction. The combined additive did not lead to any improvement in salt retention or water permeation compared to using SDS alone [206].

Jia et al. [207] studied the use of cetyltrimethylammonium chloride (CTAC) micelles as phase transfer catalysts to create PA TFC for the FO process. Their findings demonstrated that the CTAC micelles significantly influenced the PA layer, with higher CTAC concentrations resulting in enhanced salt rejection, achieving rates above 90 % for all tested salts (MgSO4 > Na2SO4 > NaCl) and up to 99.9 % for MgCl2. The researchers attributed this improvement to ionic interactions between CTAC micelles and MPD molecules in aqueous solutions, which affected the IP process of MPD and TMC, yielding a more linear molecular structure in the PA layer [207]. Despite better salt rejection, they observed that the presence of CTAC reduces water flux in TFC membranes due to the formation of a dense and microcrystalline structure in the PA layer, hindering water transport [207]. In their recent study, Jia et al. [208] used three different phase-transfer catalysts during the IP reaction in PA TFC FO membranes. They investigated the effects of dodecyl trimethyl ammonium chloride (DTAC), SDS, and dodecyl dimethyl betaine (BS12) on the molecular aggregation structures, surface morphology, and FO separation performance. The results showed that DTAC significantly improved water flux, achieving a higher water flux of 11.5 LMH with 0.1 M and 1 M NaCl solutions as feed and draw solutions, respectively; this was due to a smoother surface that mitigated the ECP effect [208]. On the other hand, SDS created a rough surface with leaf-like features that did not enhance water flux significantly due to increased ECP. Adding BS21 led to greater cross-linking, which lowered water flux but improved salt rejection. They concluded that phase-transfer catalysts are crucial in regulating the molecular structure and surface morphology of PA TFC membranes, enhancing water flux and reducing ECP for more efficient FO processes [208].

Shen and colleagues [203] employed various tertiary amine catalysts to create high-performance TFC FO membranes for desalination. They utilized three different tertiary amines: TEA, tris(2-aminoethyl) amine (TAEA), and hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA), to speed up the IP reaction, forming the PA layer of TFC membranes, thus enhancing their properties and performance. Their results indicated that all modified membranes displayed improved water flux compared to the control membrane in FO mode using a 2 M NaCl solution as the DS and DI water as the FS. This improvement was attributed to the formation of thinner PA layers. Notably, the membrane modified with 1 wt% TAEA exhibited the highest water flux due to increased surface hydrophilicity and a larger fraction of free volume within the PA matrix. However, this membrane also showed the highest RSF because of increased free volume and lower negative surface charges [203]. On the other hand, the membrane modified with 1 wt% HMTA demonstrated increased water flux, though less than that of TAEA, striking a balance with commendable water flux and the lowest specific reverse solute flux (SRSF), suggesting its potential in desalination applications due to its denser, thinner PA layer and higher cross-linking degree achieved with HMTA [203]. During fouling tests, all modified membranes showed a slower and more stable decrease in water flux compared to the control membrane, indicating improved fouling resistance against sodium alginate and gypsum scaling. Specifically, membranes modified with 3 wt% HMTA and 1 wt% TAEA performed exceptionally well, exhibiting lower flux decline ratios and higher flux recovery ratios (FRR). The enhanced antifouling properties were possibly due to smoother PA layers with higher cross-linking degrees. Furthermore, a higher cross-linking degree suggested a more complete IP reaction resulting in fewer carboxylic acid groups, which reduce foulant adhesion as these groups are sites for foulant attachment [203]. Additionally, the modified membranes demonstrated greatly enhanced chemical stability. After exposure to acid-base or chlorine treatments, they showed less variation in water flux and RSF compared to the control membrane. This enhancement was attributed to a denser PA network with a higher cross-linking degree, making the membranes more resistant to degradation [203].

Recently, Ang et al. [205] aimed to enhance the performance of NF-like FO membranes through surfactant-assisted IP. Their research investigated the effects of various surfactants, including SDS, dodecyltrimethylammonium bromide (DTAB), and sorbitan monolaurate (Span 20), on the properties and performance of the PA layer in these membranes. They discovered that SDS increased the membrane’s positive charge, resulting in low water flux due to SDS interacting with both the amine and PA layers through electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding. This interaction created a dense PA layer with a high degree of cross-linking, leading to reduced water flux. Additionally, the positively charged membrane surface facilitated salt passage, increasing the RSF [205]. Span 20, a natural surfactant with a low hydrophile-lipophile balance (HLB), was not fully soluble in water, dispersing poorly in the amine solution and resulting in weak interactions with amines and water. This led to a loose PA layer with defects that hindered membrane performance [205]. In contrast, DTAB improved the membrane's hydrophilicity and negative charge, resulting in higher water flux and lower RSF. DTAB provided optimal results in FO testing, where the membrane achieved the highest water flux of 33.69 LMH and a low RSF of 33.34 gMH using a1 M Na2SO4 solution and DI as DS and FS respectively under FO mode. The superior performance associated with DTAB was attributed to its cationic nature interacting with the PA layer, making the membrane more hydrophilic and negatively charged, thereby enhancing water flux and reducing salt passage for optimized FO membrane performance [205].

In addition to using additives with the amine solution, ionic liquids (ILs) can serve as the organic reagent phase in the IP process. Recently, water-immiscible ILs such as 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide ([C4mim][Tf2N]), were introduced to replace neurotoxic hexane in the IP process for synthesizing PA TFC membranes [209]. For example, Hartanto et al. [79] utilized ([C4mim][Tf2N]) as the IL organic phase instead of hexane in the IP process for preparing TFC FO membranes. They discovered that the resulting TFC membrane exhibited performance comparable to that of one synthesized with hexane, without needing any additives [79]. Furthermore, they mixed the IL [C4mim][Tf2N] with a more cost-effective and eco-friendly solvent, hexyl acetate, to lower the expense of preparing IL-based TFC membranes. They found that using a ([C4mim][Tf2N]/hexyl acetate mixture resulted in optimal monomer concentrations of 0.5 wt% MPD and 0.3 w/v.% TMC, which are lower than those required for traditional hexane-based IP [79]. The reduced MPD concentration in the IL phase resulted from improved transport of the MPD monomer across the water/IL interface due to the IL's amphiphilic nature [79]. Under these conditions, the TFC membrane surpassed all others, delivering a water flux of 9.1 LMH and a selectivity (SRSF) of 0.25 g/L with a 0.5 M NaCl solution as DS and DI water as FS in FO mode [79].

In summary, incorporating additives such as surfactants and phase-transfer catalysts into the IP process can facilitate the performance of PA-based TFC FO membranes. This leads to a smoother and more uniform PA layer, enhancing membrane performance and antifouling properties. While this approach enhances the cross-linking degree of the PA layer network, thereby increasing the salt rejection rate, it may also reduce water flux in certain situations. Therefore, optimizing the cross-linking degree of the PA layer is essential to achieve both high water flux and high salt rejection rates. Additionally, using ionic liquids as alternatives to traditional organic solvents can lower the environmental impact and costs of membrane production. Overall, modifying with these additives could be a straightforward and effective method to create more efficient, durable, and eco-friendly PA TFC membranes without significant cost increases. Finally, the potential role of these additives in the FO process has not been fully explored, making further research highly necessary.

The microporous support layer is vital for both the development and performance of the PA TFC FO membranes. The most essential characteristics of the supporting membrane layer include its hydrophilicity, pore size, surface porosity, and overall porosity [84,159]. To illustrate, a support layer with a hydrophilic surface enables a greater volume of the MPD solution to infiltrate its pores, creating an inward-curved meniscus that slows the escape of MPD during the IP process. This leads to a more evenly distributed PA layer that extends deeper into the pores of the support.
