Conversely, a hydrophobic surface on the support layer causes less MPD solution to enter, resulting in an outward-curved meniscus inside the pores and rapid MPD loss during the IP. This typically causes a thicker and more uneven PA layer to form. [210].

Similarly, other crucial factors like pore size and surface porosity have significant effects on PA development. Support layers characterized by finer pores and lower porosity typically yield a comparatively thin PA layer with sparsely spaced small bubbles. On the other hand, a support membrane with larger pores and higher surface porosity tends to encourage larger PA bubble formation. These bubbles pack tightly together, forming a denser, thicker PA layer [162,163]. Another aspect worth noting is that higher overall porosity within the membrane support enhances water flux, reduces the S parameter, and diminishes the effect of ICP.

Thus, fine-tuning properties such as hydrophilicity, pore size, surface porosity and overall porosity of the membrane support may improve PA formation processes and influence the overall efficacy of TFC FO membranes. These elements directly affect water permeability, selectivity, and membrane stability [39]. Striking an appropriate balance among these aspects is critical for tailoring membrane attributes to specific needs. Subsequent sections will offer insights into prevalently used methods for altering membrane support properties, including blending with nanomaterials, and integrating pore-forming additives.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Incorporation of nanomaterials in support layers
ce:section.ce:para: Besides incorporating nanomaterials into the active layer, altering the substrate of TFC FO membranes using nanomaterials has also gained interest in numerous research works [211]. Generally, a substrate modified with nanomaterials is fabricated through a physical blending, which is the simplest and most widely used preparation technique, with a polymer dope solution before undergoing the phase inversion process [37], as shown in Fig. 15.( Fig. 16).

In addition to the advancement of TFN FO membrane development, previous research has indicated that the incorporation of nanomaterials into the support layer significantly enhances permeability, selectivity, water flux, salt rejection, and antifouling properties [47]. Generally, integrating hydrophilic nanomaterials into the polymer matrix of the substrate results in a structure with increased porosity, hydrophilicity, and reduced tortuosity [4], while also improving thermal, chemical, and mechanical stability [213]. Furthermore, it increases the solvent exchange rate between the solvent and nonsolvent during phase inversion, preventing sponge pore formation and promoting finger-pore structure with low tortuosity development within the substrate membranes [166,167]. This typically lowers the structural parameter of FO membranes, which aids in diminishing the effect of ICP. The dispersion and incorporation of nanomaterials directly into the substrate layer not only alters the morphology and chemical attributes of the substrate itself but also significantly impacts PA formation [214].

As mentioned in Section 4.2, factors such as size, dispersity, shape, and compatibility of nanomaterials have an impact on the performance of the created membrane. The use of solid and porous nanoparticles, as well as 1D and 2D nanomaterials, are also employed in the construction of membrane substrates. Generally, incorporating nanomaterials like TiO2 nanoparticles [215], porous zeolite nanoparticles [216], 1D carbon nanofibers (CNFs) [102], and 2D GO nanosheets [106], can influence the porosity and hydrophilicity of the substrate while lowering the S values for TFC FO membranes [39]. However, solid nanoparticles may compromise the integrity of active layer due to potential nanoparticles agglomerating on the substrate surface. On the other hand, 1D and 2D nanomaterials demonstrate better compatibility with the substrate matrix because of their high aspect ratios [137].

Table 3 summarizes the research conduct on nanomaterials incorporated TFC FO membrane substrates with their FO performance in comparison to the pristine membrane. The table clearly shows that the modified TFC membranes with the lowest structural parameters exhibit a higher water flux than that of the unmodified membrane. It is important to acknowledge, despite varied results regarding the RSF, that in certain instances, RSF may show a substantial difference, while in others, it remains relatively similar when comparing the modified membranes to their unmodified counterparts.

Salehi et al. [216] developed a nanocomposite substrate by incorporating modified natural zeolite (Clinoptilolite) into a PES substrate matrix through phase inversion, aiming to improve the desalination performance of TFC FO membranes. They discovered that utilizing 0.4 wt% led to increased surface and overall porosity and promoted the development of macrovoids finger-like structures, resulting in a reduction in the S parameter. These changes minimized the ICP effect, which impedes water transport across the membrane, and consequently enhanced both the water flux of the substrate and overall performance of the composite membrane. They explained that the zeolite nanoparticle facilitated the phase inversion process by drawing more water from the coagulation bath, resulting in increased membrane porosity, especially at lower concentration of zeolite nanoparticles. However, when zeolite nanoparticle concentration was increased to 0.6 %, it negatively affected the membrane performance due to reduced cross-linking within the PA selective layer, resulting from zeolite nanoparticle agglomeration on the PES substrate [216]. In another study, Sirinupong et al. [215] incorporated TiO2 nanoparticles, GO nanosheets, and TiO2/GO hybrid nanomaterials into a PSF substrate to enhance its hydrophilicity, roughness, porosity, water flux, and overall performance of the TFC membrane. The findings revealed that TFC membranes composed of TiO2, GO, and TiO2/GO substrates displayed superior water flux and reduced structure parameters compared to the control membrane. The best performance and lowest S parameter were observed in the TFC membrane made of PSF-TiO2/GO substrate. The presence of long finger-like voids extending from top to bottom was evident in TiO2 and TiO2/GO substrates, while the control and GO substrates exhibited shorter finger-like structures supported by a macrovoid sublayer [215].

Zhang et al. [108] utilized phosphorylated TiO2 (p-TiO2) functionalized nanoparticles to modify the polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) substrate, increasing its hydrophilicity and porosity. This modification allowed for the uniform and sufficient absorption of MPD molecules. They claim that the p-TiO2 nanoparticles were rich in phosphate groups which strongly interacted with MPD molecules, limiting their migration toward the organic phase during the IP process and creating a denser, smoother PA layer. Moreover, the p-TiO2 nanoparticles enhanced the antifouling capability of the TFC FO membrane, with water flux reduction due to fouling dropping from 31.3 % in the control membrane to 16 % in the optimal membrane [108]. In Further research on functionalizing nanomaterials, the study by Tavakol et al. [102] utilized carboxyl functionalized carbon nanofibers (f-CNFs). Their findings revealed that f-CNFs enhanced the hydrophilicity, porosity, and mechanical strength of the PSF substrate while also reducing the ICP effect by lowering the S parameter of the TFC FO membrane. The optimal membrane containing 0.3 wt% f-CNFs demonstrated increased porosity and decreased thickness compared to the control membrane, resulting in the lowest S parameter of approximately 788 µm. This was attributed to the hydrophilic nature of f-CNFs, which accelerated solvent and non-solvent exchange during phase inversion, resulting in a more porous structure and causing the membrane to shrink further before phase inversion was complete, resulting in reduced thickness. Additionally, the f-CNFs influenced the development of the PA rejection layer on the substrate surface, leading to a denser and more uniform PA layer with increased water permeability and salt rejection [102].

Recently, Zhang et al., [222], hydrophobic carbon dots (HCDs) were used as nanofillers in the polyacrylonitrile (PAN) support layer to regulate water channels and create a more efficient FO membrane. The incorporation of HCDs into the support layer resulted in enhanced porosity and permeability by encouraging the development of cohesive finger-like hierarchical channels and micro-convex structures, as well as integrating the PA active layer. Moreover, it minimized the ICP effect due to the lower S parameters of the HCD-induced water channels. The HCD-modified TFC membrane with 10 wt% had superior separation properties and permselectivity, exhibiting higher water flux and lower RSF compared to the control TFC membrane [222]. More recently, Yu et al. [226] employed sulfonated graphene oxide (SGO) to modify the PSF in a PA TFC FO membrane for removing fluoride ions from high-fluoride wastewater. Their findings revealed that incorporating SGO into the membrane support increased hydrophilicity due to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups like sulfonate and hydroxyl [226]. This enhancement made the membrane surface more water-affinitive and reduced contact angle, resulting in a higher water flux. The SGO-modified membrane created a thin and porous layer, increasing the porosity and roughness of the TFC FO membrane. Moreover, the negatively charged SGO lowered the surface potential of the PA TFC membrane, significantly improving fluoride ion (F-) retention in high-water flux FO membranes up to 83.2 % [226].

In summary, integrating nanomaterials into the membrane support of TFC FO membranes can enhance its hydrophilicity, porosity, and pore size. This enhancement can lead to a reduction of membrane structural parameters, thus reducing the ICP and boosting water flux. Furthermore, it can modify the surface properties and morphology of the membrane support, facilitating the creation of a dense and uniform PA layer without defects. This leads to an increase in the water permeance and solute selectivity of the membrane. Nonetheless, suppose these nanomaterials are not well dispersed, compatible, or stable in the membrane matrix. In that case, they may affect the membrane performance negatively, which in turn can hinder the creation of a free defect active layer. Consequently, it is essential to choose suitable nanomaterials and fine-tune their concentrations to resolve these issues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Blending pore forming additives into support layer
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the integration of nanomaterials into the support layer, it should be emphasized that adding of pore forming additives into the dope solution can significantly influence the morphology and the development of pores in membrane substrates during the phase inversion process [39,183], subsequently improving the performance of the TFC FO membranes. The role of these additives in the polymer mixture is crucial as they have a considerable effect on both thermodynamics, including affinity with solvent, nonsolvent, and polymer, as well as solvent-nonsolvent solubility, and on kinetics by increasing the viscosity of the casting solution [228].

Hydrophilic polymers such as Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), polyether glycol (PEG) and poly (vinyl chloride) (PVC), along with inorganic salts commonly used as pore forming additives, facilitate the development of pores and structure in the membrane support [47]. Notably, PVP and PEG stand out as the most prevalent hydrophilic polymer additives due to their well-discussed contribution to membrane performance [135]. This is achieved by improving the membrane permeation characteristics via changes in both the viscosity of the casting solution and the exchange rate between solvent and nonsolvent during the phase inversion [133], leading to the formation of large macrovoids and distinct, finger-like pores throughout the support matrix of the membrane [229]. Moreover, these additives can impact the diffusion rate of MPD from within pores to the IP interface, which may result in thicker and denser PA layers [79,84].

Wu et al. [230] examined the impact of PEG-400 on the structure and performance of the TFC FO membranes used for water desalination. They incorporated PEG-400 as an additive in the fabrication of PSF support and explored the effect of varying concentrations of PEG-400 on membrane characteristics and performance. It was observed that the introduction of PEG-400 improved the support layer by making it more hydrophilic, increasing porosity, and altering both pore size and shape. These alterations played a significant role in decreasing internal concentration polarization as well as in enhancing water flux [230]. An optimal 6 % weight concentration of PEG-400 was identified for achieving higher water flux due to larger pore formation within the support structure. At this optimal concentration, they recorded a membrane water flux rate of 47.4 LMH and a reverse salt flux rate of 7.6 gMH, with 2.0 M NaCl as the draw solution and DI as the feed solution [230]. In another study, Zheng et al. [98] presented the development of a high-efficiency and economical TFC FO membrane utilizing a substrate composed of SPSF/PVC. Their research indicated that blending SPSF into the PVC substrate enhanced not only its porosity and hydrophilic properties but also the substrate water permeability [98]. Moreover, this positively affected the TFC membrane structure and overall performance. They determined that a 2.5 % blend ratio of SPSF to PVC was optimal, yielding a TFC membrane capable of achieving a water flux of 25.53/48.37 LMH and a specific salt flux of 0.10/0.09 g/L in FO and PRO modes, respectively, when a draw solution of 1 M NaCl and a feed solution of DI were used [98].

Bao et al. [14] examined how the introduction of PVP and PEG in SPES membrane support the characteristics of the PA layer, the overall performance of the membranes, and their fouling behaviors when using domestic wastewater. They observed that PVP integration leads to the development of a finger-like structure within the substrate, characterized by large macropores. Conversely, adding PEG results in the creation of a homogenous, sponge-type structure marked by smaller pores on the surface. Membranes with added PVP encouraged the creation of a rough PA layer with notable defects and substantial PA growth within the support pores. In contrast, membranes with added PEG were conducive to forming a thin, smooth PA layer with a densely cross-linked inner structure. Comparatively, membranes containing 1 wt% of PVP showed enhanced water flux (38.18 LMH) but were less effective at solute selectivity (0.282 g/L) than those containing 10 wt% of PEG (33.58 LMH and 0.160 g/L). The reason for this is that the larger pores in the PVP-modified substrate reduce ICP but simultaneously increase the thickness and mass transfer resistance within the PA layers. On the other hand, the sponge-like pore structure in PEG-modified substrates enhances both water transport and solute selectivity in the PA layer, even though it faces a higher ICP [14]. Upon examining solute rejection from wastewater, they found that membranes modified with PEG demonstrated a superior ability to reject organic compounds as indicated by higher removal rates for Total Organic Carbon (TOC) at 97.48 % and PO4 ³ ⁻-P at 98.53 %, compared to those achieved by their PVP-containing counterparts at 93.01 % and 93.71 %, respectively. This is likely because of the denser and smoother PA layers characteristic of the PEG-modified membranes. However, both membranes show poor rejection rates for NH4 + -N (less than 59.06 %), which is primarily due to the significant resemblance in polarity and hydraulic size between NH4 and water molecules. Additionally, the membranes with PVP demonstrate a reduced tendency towards fouling and more easily reversed fouling when compared to those with added PEG. This can be ascribed to the increase in roughness and the presence of carboxylic groups on the polyamide layer, which promote the absorption of organic contaminants and their chemical adherence [14].

It may be possible to blend pore forming additives with other hydrophilic polymers to enhance their hydrophilicity and the development of pores within membrane structures. Ma et al. [231], for instance, discovered that the addition of polyvinyl butyral (PVB), a hydrophilic polymer, into cellulose acetate butyrate (CAB) support layers of TFC FO membranes, combined with a PEG pore former, enhanced hydrophilicity as well as the formation of pores within the support structure. By Changing PVB concentration from 0 to 4 wt% in conjunction with 10 wt% PEG altered the membrane structure from a sponge-like structure to one featuring finger-like and cellular pores. This change influenced vital factors such as porosity, size of the pores, their interconnectivity and the overall resistance to mass transfer in the membrane. A concentration of 2 wt% was identified as optimal for PVB, producing a support layer with extensive porosity, larger pores that are well connected, and reduced resistance to mass transfer [231]. These characteristics facilitate both increased water flux and a minimized ICP effect during FO processes. The resulting TFC FO membrane showcased superior performance, achieving the highest recorded water flux of 27.5 LMH using a 1 M NaCl draw solution under AL-DS mode, an enhancement of 40 % over commercial CTA membranes and 130 % over membranes made solely from CAB [231]. Most recently, Shafie et al. [232] evaluated the performance of TFC FO membranes that incorporated PVP with different molecular weights as a pore-forming additive. The PES substrate layer was created through phase inversion, utilizing three distinct PVP molecular weights (10 kDa, 40 kDa, and 360 kDa). The study explored how blending PES with both low and high-molecular-weight PVP impacted the membrane's performance and morphology. It was determined that a PVP molecular weight of 40 kDa was the most effective additive for the PES membrane substrate, demonstrating the highest porosity at 86.00 % ± 0.55 and the lowest water contact angle at 41.87° ± 3.43. Additionally, the water flux of PES/PVP40k exhibited the highest water flux (3.9310 LMH) with the lowest RSD (0.76 gMH) when using 1 M NaCl as DS and DI as FS under FO mode. These findings highlight the crucial role of 40 kDa PVP in forming TFC FO membranes with microporous structures, leading to superior performance and reduced ICP effects [232].

In addition to macromolecule additives, pore formation in membranes can also be induced by incorporating inorganic salts such as NaCl and LiCl. Darwish et al. [233] explored the use of LiCl as a pore-forming agent within three types of polymer membrane supports: PSF, PES, and polyphenylsulfone (PPSU), significantly speeds up pore creation and boosts the interconnectivity of pores, leading to enhance the performance of TFC FO membranes for water desalination. Their findings revealed that every membrane developed an asymmetric structure when LiCl was mixed into the casting solution. This structure included a compact top layer and a bottom layer with substantial, finger-like pores [233]. Additionally, the water flux for these membranes rose upon adding LiCl. Specifically, in AL-FS mode, water flux escalated from 3.59 to 6.71 LMH. for PSF, 2.85 to 6.88 LMH for PES, and from 3.04 to 5.72 LMH for PPSU. When switched to AL-DS mode, it improved from 5.09 to 7.29 LMH for PSF, from 3.77 to 10.67 LMH for PES, and from 4.13 to 8.48 LMH for PPSU, respectively. However, these LiCl-enhanced membranes exhibited increased RSF over their counterparts without LiCl, as well as greater SRSF following the addition of LiCl [233]. Another approach to tailoring the characteristics of the support layer is to introduce them into the coagulation bath during the phase inversion process instead of incorporating salts into the casting solution. For example, Shen et al. [234] created a TFC membrane with a low polymer concentration (4 wt%), using polyacrylonitrile (PAN) as the substrate, by implementing phase inversion in a NaCl aqueous solution. This was done to produce a membrane with a reduced S parameter and to minimize the impact of ICP. The resulting membrane exhibited an increase in porosity and developed finger-like pore structures; it also had significantly lowered S parameter values when compared to the control membrane (decreasing from 560.46 to 84.35 µm). Furthermore, the modified TFC membranes displayed improved FO performance, with water fluxes that were 7.8–31.4 % higher and RSF that was 14.9–49.0 % lower than those of the original control membranes [234].

It is essential to highlight that incorporating pore-forming agents along with nanomaterials into the casting solution can significantly enhance water flux without sacrificing the RSF. This is achieved by promoting the development of straighter, more elongated finger-like pores within the membrane support [235]. In particular, the combination of these additives and nanomaterials can lead to greater porosity in the substrate as well as increased hydrophilicity, thereby boosting amine monomer retention, which typically leads to better membrane water permeability [236]. Additionally, the introduction of macromolecules can prevent nanoparticles from agglomerating on the membrane surface, which contributes to the stabilization of its structural integrity [237]. Several studies are mentioned in Section 5.1, Table 3 demonstrates the use of pore-forming additives with nanomaterials such as Zeolite/PVP [216], SiO2 @MWNTs/PVP [217], GO/PEG [106], CNPs/PEG [218], f-CNFs/PVP [102], GNPs/PEG [223].

All in all, adding pore-forming additives can help develop finger-like pore structures in the support layer of TFC FO membranes, which in turn enhances the efficiency of these membranes by increasing water flux and mitigating the impact of ICP. Further, the addition of these additives can influence the IP process and the characteristics of the selective layer, including its thickness, structure, and degree of cross-linking. More importantly, the incorporation of these additives combined with nanomaterials could strongly impact the structure of the membrane support and enhance the overall performance of the membranes.

Efforts in FO technology have shifted towards interlayer construction to enhance the TFC membrane performance [103,191]. The substrate's porous structure near the PA regions is crucial for amine monomer capture during the IP process. An innovative "gutter layer" interlayer atop the substrate improves mechanical stability, promotes thin, defect-free polyamide layer formation, and enhances transport efficiency by creating faster pathways for water molecules, as depicted in Fig. 17. Different interlayer constructions influence amine monomer diffusion rates, leading to controlled IP reactions [194–196]. Interlayers, categorized as organic coatings, nanomaterial, or nanocomposite, prevent polyamide intrusion into the porous substrate, ensuring uniform amine monomer distribution and improving water permeability [197,198,201]. Various materials such as polydopamine (PDA), TA, CNTs, GO, nanoparticles (NPs), MOF, and GQDs have been used, offering membranes antimicrobial, anti-fouling, chlorine resistance, thermal stability, and mechanical strength [103,191,196].

Table 4 presents a comprehensive summary of the progress made in incorporating interlayers into TFC-FO membranes, along with their associated performances and potential limitations. The significance of polydopamine (PDA), an interlayer material combined with other nanomaterials, in enhancing TFC-FO membranes is evident across several studies. Han et al. [239] employed PDA as an interlayer between PSF substrates and a modified PA layer, resulting in TFC-FO membranes with enhanced water permeability and salt rejection, as shown in Fig. 18 [239]. Lu et al. incorporated PDA-induced LDH nanoparticles to improve chlorine and fouling resistance, enhancing membrane performance [204–206]. Shah et al. [240] introduced a TFC membrane with a PDA/halloysite nanotube (HNT) interlayer, achieving exceptional water flux without compromising reverse salt flux. Similarly, Choi et al. [241] also developed a TFC-FO membrane with a PDA/GO interlayer, significantly increasing water flux while maintaining solute flux. These studies collectively highlight the versatility and effectiveness of PDA in enhancing the performance of TFC-FO membranes, offering promising advancements for various water treatment and desalination applications.

In another study, Gonzales et al. [243] utilized PDA through self-polymerization of dopamine to create interlayers in TFC-FO/PRO membranes. Two types of PDA interlayers —smooth and particulate—and their effects were explored on membrane morphology and performance. Results indicated enhanced water flux with both interlayer types. pH levels notably influenced particulate PDA formation, affecting PA structure. Nanoparticulate PDA interlayers facilitated more porous PA formation, enhancing membrane performance. In FO testing, membranes with nanoparticulate PDA at pH 9.0 exhibited the highest initial water flux, although with compromised reverse solute flux [243]. Most recently, Li and his team (2022) addressed the challenges posed by the high roughness and large pore size of electrospun nanofiber membrane (ENM) substrates by introducing a smooth and hydrophilic PDA interlayer onto a PVDF-ENM [244]. This interlayer was found to facilitate the formation of a thinner, more cross-linked, and less defective PA active layer on the ENM substrate. FO desalination tests demonstrated the significant effectiveness of the PDA interlayer in improving the performance of the electrically enhanced eTFC-FO membrane. Remarkably high-water flux and ions rejection were achieved during desulfurization wastewater desalination tested in FO mode [244]. The reduced defects in the PA layer due to the PDA interlayer significantly enhanced selectivity and anti-fouling performance to high salinity ions, e.g., Ca2 + , Mg2+, and SO4 2-, Na+, and Cl-. In comparison, PDA coating on each nanofiber within the PVDF-ENM substrate was also prepared and assessed for the eTFC-FO membrane, but this approach did not address the drawbacks of the rough and large-pore-sized ENM substrate for PA preparation [244].

Hung et al. [245] developed TFC-FO membranes using ultrathin GO laminated interlayers, adjusting interlayer spacing with crosslinking agents including the MPD and TMC. Covalent bonds between layers and crosslinking agents minimized d-spacing stretching. The asymmetric structure with GO-MPD/TMC showed the highest flux and low reverse salt flux in PRO mode, maintaining consistent water flux and suppressing ICP during long-term operation [245]. In another study, Yu et al. [252] created a TFC-FO membrane on a hydrophobic PVDF nanofiber substrate using a GO and oxidized multi-walled carbon nanotubes (OMWCNTs) interlayer via IP as shown in Fig. 18 [252]. The interlayer composition, optimized with a mass ratio of GO to OMWCNTs at 2:1, influenced substrate morphology and roughness, enhancing hydrophilicity for water-phase monomer diffusion during IP. The interlayer reduced the PA layer thickness by 60 %, increasing layer spacing and forming water transport channels. The optimized membrane exhibited exceptionally high water flux and low salt flux in both FO and PRO modes due to a lower S parameter [252]. Recently, Wu et al. [246] also utilized GO nanosheets for the formation of an interlayer to modify hydrophilic polyacrylonitrile (PAN) electrospun nanofiber substrates. Subsequently, the PA active layer was formed through the IP of MPD and TMC. It was observed that the structure and properties, including osmotic flux and specific salt flux, of the PA active layer were effectively adjusted by varying the density of GO loading [246]. Optimal loading of GO nanosheets was found to hinder PA penetration into the surface pores of the substrate while simultaneously enhancing the cross-linking degree of the PA active layer, leading to improvements in water flux and salt rejection. The optimized FO membrane (28 μg/cm2 - GO) exhibited higher water flux and lower specific salt flux compared to the control membrane in FO mode. The appropriate loading of GO nanosheets on electrospun nanofiber substrates was demonstrated to be a competitive strategy for developing high-performance TFC-FO membranes based on electrospun nanofibers [246].( Fig. 19).

Zhou et al. and Yang et al. both explored the integration of CNTs as spray-coated interlayers in TFC-FO membranes [194,211]. Zhou et al. [247] investigated an ultrathin CNT interlayer's influence on the PA layer structure and transport behavior, finding that it facilitated the formation of a highly permeable and selective PA layer while preventing undesired PA structure development within substrate pores. This resulted in significantly higher water flux without compromising salt rejection rates compared to previously reported FO membranes [247]. Similarly, Yang et al. [250] demonstrated that a spray-coated CNTs interlayer facilitated a more favorable interface for PA growth, leading to continuous and rough PA layer formation and enhanced water transport. The modified membranes in both studies exhibited substantially improved water flux and reduced reverse salt flux compared to unmodified membranes [250]. Additionally, Zhang et al. and Sun et al. introduced innovative interlayer strategies for TFC-FO membranes [215,261]. Zhang et al. [251] employed a TiO2/CNT nanocomposite interlayer on mullite ceramic substrates, enhancing membrane surface properties, reducing pore size, and improving water flux and salt rejection compared to membranes without interlayers. Sun et al. [254] also developed a novel Ti3C2Tx MXene /CNTs interlayer, which enhanced interfacial degassing, amine monomer sorption, and PA rejection layer properties, leading to a four-fold increase in water flux and improved salt rejection. Both studies demonstrated promising improvements in membrane performance, highlighting the potential of these interlayer designs for enhancing FO membrane efficiency. In advancing research conducted by Wang et al. [214], a TFC-FO membrane incorporating a CNT interlayer characterized by high electrical conductivity, water permeability, and self-cleaning capabilities aimed at enhancing FO operations. Considering the electro-generated oxidative species, particularly the reactive chlorine species produced from locally accumulated chlorine ions, the resultant TFC-FO membrane effectively removed deposited bacterial cells and extracellular polymeric substances from the membrane surface, ensuring sustained membrane performance. Applying an optimized voltage of 2.5 V to the fouled membrane restored 85 % of its original water flux and maintained stable performance over three operating cycles [253]. The effective membrane cleaning was primarily attributed to the in-situ generation of reactive chlorine species via electro-oxidation of locally enriched Cl-, accompanied by direct oxidation of foulants by the conductive membrane. Their findings highlight the significant potential of the electrochemically conductive CNT interlayer approach within the TFC-FO membrane for mitigating biofouling, with potential applicability to other membrane-based desalination and wastewater treatment processes [253].

Several studies have explored the use of alternative interlayers to enhance the performance of TFC-FO membranes. Razavi et al. [249] developed a triple-layer TFC-FO membrane by applying MPD and TMC via IP on a tannic acid-Fe3+ (TA-Fe3+)-coated microfiltration (MF) PVDF substrate. The TA-Fe3+ interlayer enhanced substrate hydrophilicity and facilitated the formation of a thin, smooth PA film with reduced defects, resulting in tripled water flux and lower reverse solute flux compared to the control membrane [249]. He et al. [255] synthesized a hydrophilic sulfonated covalent organic framework (COF) and used it as an interlayer in TFC-FO membranes. Sulfonation of the COFs improved hydrophilicity, introduced negative charge, and reduced pore size, facilitating water permeation while maintaining high salt rejection. The PA/sulfonated COF/TMC-nylon membrane showed enhanced water flux, salt rejection, and selectivity in FO mode [255]. Cheng et al. [248] developed a high-performance FO membrane using an electrospun PAN substrate with an HNTs interlayer. The modified HNTs-filled substrate pores improved the IP interface and enhanced water transport, resulting in increased water flux and decreased reverse salt flux compared to control membranes [248]. Ma et al. [258] also employed PVDF fine nanofibers as an interlayer in TFC-FO membranes. The PVDF nanofiber interlayer reduced the S parameter, enhancing the membrane hydrophilicity and permeability, and improved anti-fouling performance against sodium alginate (SA), achieving a high flux recovery ratio. These studies collectively demonstrate the effectiveness of various interlayer materials, including TA-Fe3+, sulfonated COFs, halloysite nanotubes, and PVDF nanofibers, in enhancing the performance of TFC-FO membranes by improving substrate properties, facilitating IP, and enhancing water transport and selectivity [258].

Wang et al. [139] and Wang et al. [257] each introduced innovative approaches using MOFs, specifically UiO-66 and MoS2 @NH2-UiO-66, respectively, in TFC-FO membranes. Wang et al. [139] decorated UiO-66 fillers within the interlayer of TFC-FO membranes, as depicted in Fig. 20, significantly enhancing water permeability, salt rejection, and reducing the structural parameter compared to control membranes. This approach yielded superior separation performance with only 5% UiO-66 fillers, attributing the improvements to increased surface hydrophilicity, effective contact areas, and enhanced rejection barriers from the decorated UiO-66 fillers [139]. Similarly, Wang et al. [257] also integrated MoS2 @NH2-UiO-66 nanocomposites to fabricate a PA-TFC-FO membrane for treating low-concentration ions and small hydration radius nuclide ions. Incorporating MoS2 @NH2-UiO-66 enhanced the IP reaction concentration of MPD monomer and crosslinking degree of PA on the nanomaterial surface, preventing TMC accumulation in substrate pores. This modification significantly increased water flux by 73.31% and reduced reverse salt flux by 68.18%, surpassing other TFC membranes while demonstrating excellent fouling retention rates for various ions [257]. Liu et al. [256] similarly developed an interlayered TFC-FO membrane using aluminum tetra-(4-carboxyphenyl)-porphyrin (Al-MOF) nanosheets uniformly distributed on a macro-porous substrate. This approach established a flat and moderate hydrophilic reaction interface, leading to a thin PA layer devoid of substrate penetration, featuring abundant leaf-like structures. The optimized Al-MOF-TFC membrane exhibited enhanced water permeability, reduced reverse sodium flux, exceptional stability, and sustained high permeability even under challenging conditions including high salinity and contamination. These studies collectively demonstrate the effectiveness of MOF-based interlayers in TFC-FO membrane design, enhancing separation performance, water permeability, salt rejection, and fouling resistance for various applications in water treatment and ion separation [256].

Most recently, Ren et al. [259] investigated the fabrication of CTA-TFC-FO membranes using a spray-modified non-solvent induced phase separation (s-NIPS) method. The PA layer was optimized by incorporating a PDA/GO-Fe3+-TA interlayer onto a dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO)-based PA membrane, resulting in high-throughput and highly selective TFC membranes. These CTA membranes exhibited large pore size and high porosity, achieving a water flux surpassing that of HTI commercial membranes by 5.6 times with lower reverse salt flux, demonstrating exceptional separation performance suitable for practical applications [259]. Similarly, Zhang et al. [260] also developed three-layer TFC-FO membranes comprising a CS/TA co-deposition interlayer between a PVDF substrate and a PA active layer, as illustrated graphically in Fig. 21. The CS/TA interlayer, rich in alcohol hydroxyl and amino groups, enhanced water molecule attraction and modulated the IP reaction of the PA active layer, resulting in a more uniform membrane surface [260]. Compared to pristine TFC membranes, TFC-CS/TA membranes exhibited notably higher permeation flux and reduced reverse salt flux. The optimized TFC-CS/TA-FO membrane showed superior performance with increased water flux, decreased reverse salt flux, and higher fluoride ion retention rate. This novel approach holds promise for industrial fluorine-containing wastewater treatment, showcasing efficient TFC FO membrane fabrication [260].

In summary, recent efforts have been focused on advancing inter-layered TFC-FO membranes for diverse water treatment applications. Various functional materials including PDA, TA-Fe3 + , GO, CNT, MXene, and metal and covalent organic frameworks have been utilized in the fabrication of these membranes. These innovative TFC-FO membranes have demonstrated significantly improved water flux without compromising rejection or selectivity, addressing the traditional trade-off between productivity and efficiency. Interlayers situated between the substrate and the selective layer play a crucial role in regulating substrate surface properties. Nanostructured interlayers enhance substrate properties such as uniformity, pore size reduction, adjustable surface roughness, and favorable wettability, facilitating IP and resulting in a thinner and more uniform separation layer with improved selectivity. The integration of interlayers is considered a viable approach to modulating the reaction interface while preserving the well-defined macro-porous structure of the support material with minimal material loss. However, further research is needed to address gaps in understanding long-term permeation performance in high-salinity environments, anti-fouling capacity, and chemical stability and durability. These critical aspects require further investigation to fully exploit the potential advantages offered by interlayers in TFC-FO membranes.

A double layered TFC FO membrane consists of a porous sublayer sandwiched between two dense rejection layers, with one facing the DS and the other facing the FS [262], as shown in Fig. 22. In FO mode, ICP typically causes a significant reduction in water flux [67], [263]. Nevertheless, foulants are unable to penetrate the porous support. While ICP for PRO mode is drastically reduced, foulants tend to penetrate the porous support easily [264]. Thus, a double-skinned FO membrane has been designed and fabricated to incorporate the advantages of both modes [262], [265]. In this design, a dense layer facing the DS serves to prevent RSD, while the second layer facing the FS restricts foulants from penetrating the porous substrate. This double-skinned membrane design also helps to reduce ICP, as the solute solution is unable to reach the support layer of the membrane directly [37]. Therefore, double-skinned membranes are regarded as a significant advancement in TFC FO membranes for addressing fouling and concentration polarization issues without changing the PRO mode orientation where a higher water flux can be achieved [11]. Various methods have been employed to create double-skinned TFC-FO membranes, including PDA deposition, IP, and LbL polyelectrolyte deposition [40], with the latter being the most effective for fabricating double-skinned FO membranes [123].

Table 5 provides an extensive overview of advancements in integrating double-skinned layers into TFC FO membranes, highlighting their performance metrics and possible constraints. In 2010, Wang et al. pioneered the development of double-skinned cellulose triacetate CTA-TFC membranes FO processes, achieving higher water flux and acceptable RSF using DI water as the FS and 5.0 M MgCl2 as the DS in PRO mode. The addition of a second skin layer, although potentially increasing water transport resistance, significantly alleviated the negative impact of ICP [266]. Further studies by Zhou & Lee provided a viability analysis of double-skinned TFC-FO membranes using a mathematical approach, indicating that while double-skinned membranes exhibit superior anti-fouling properties, these membranes do not generally outperform single-skinned membranes in other aspects [267].

In the same context, significant advancements have been made in the development of double-skinned TFC-FO membranes for improved water treatment applications. Duong et al. [268] pioneered the fabrication of flat sheet CA membranes with a selective PA layer synthesized via IP as the primary skin, complemented by a secondary skin composed of a sulfonated pentablock copolymer (Nexar copolymer) for emulsified oil particle rejection. These double-skinned membranes exhibited reduced ICP and fouling tendencies compared to single-skinned membranes, with notable enhancements in water flux and reduced reverse salt transport in FO experiments [268]. Building on this, Song et al. [269] introduced novel double-skinned TFC-FO membranes incorporating CNTs through IP on a PSf substrate, as depicted in Fig. 23. The inclusion of CNTs significantly influenced membrane properties, enhancing solute rejection and water flux compared to single-layered TFC membranes. The optimal TFC membrane with CNTs demonstrated a remarkable increase in water flux and enhanced antifouling capacity [269]. In 2016, Liu et al. [270] designed a double-skinned membrane integrating PDA surface deposition on the bottom surface and a PA layer via IP on the top surface of the substrate, focusing on preventing fouling. Although PDA deposition reduced pure water permeability (PWP), the modified double-layered TFC-FO membrane effectively blocked foulant entry and demonstrated superior water flux recovery in PRO mode after cleaning [270]. Subsequently, Ong et al. [271] adapted the bottom surface of TFC membranes using a hydrophilic zwitterionic polymer to treat oily wastewater while forming a top PA selective layer. The zwitterionic polymer layer acted as an antifouling barrier, resulting in higher water flux and improved water recovery rate compared to single-layer membranes in FO mode [271]. These studies collectively highlight the progress in developing double-skinned TFC-FO membranes with enhanced performance attributes, including improved water flux, solute rejection, antifouling properties, and reduced internal fouling tendencies, contributing significantly to advancing the practical feasibility of FO processes for water treatment applications [271].

In 2019, significant progress was made in the development of double-skinned TFC-FO membranes with enhanced performance attributes. Yang et al. [272] explored the impact of varying draw solute concentrations within a double-skinned membrane using IP. Their membrane design incorporated a hydrophilic nylon substrate layer sandwiched between two selective skin layers—one containing NaCl concentrations mixed with MPD and another thick skin layer without NaCl [272]. The study demonstrated that increasing solute concentration up to a certain point improved water flux by altering the effective driving force between active layers. Immersing the membrane in a 0.45 M NaCl solution enhanced water flow by 14.58% compared to membranes without NaCl immersion, with stable salt rejection rates and superior fouling resistance compared to single-skinned membranes [272]. Similarly, Zhang et al. [273] developed an antifouling double-skinned FO membrane for wastewater applications, featuring a PA salt-rejecting layer on a PK substrate and a fouling-resistant layer composed of poly(sulfobetaine methacrylate) (PSBMA) combined with MWCNT (MWCNT/PSBMA) on the bottom side (see Fig. 24). The study incorporated IP for the PA top layer and ATRP for grafting PSBMA onto MWCNT, which was then coated onto the PK substrate. This design effectively increased hydrophilicity, minimized pore size, and demonstrated superior resistance to organic fouling. The resulting double-skinned membrane (TFC-MWCNT/PSBMA) exhibited a minimal decline in water flux and a high recovery rate of up to 82% compared to control membranes, highlighting its potential for practical application in water treatment [273].

In recent studies, advancements have been made in membrane design to enhance water flux and anti-fouling capabilities. Deng et al. [274] developed a sandwich-like TFC-FO membrane featuring CNTs on both sides of a PES substrate as shown in Fig. 25. By depositing PDA-modified SWCNTs (PDA-SWCNTs) via vacuum filtration, the membrane exhibited excellent perm-selectivity and anti-fouling properties in both FO and PRO modes. The CNT's top layer facilitated the formation of the PA layer using the IP method. In contrast, the back layer of CNTs contributed to increased rejection rates for foulants such as bovine serum albumin (BSA) and enhanced hydrophilicity. This TFC-CNT-PES-CNT membrane demonstrated effective anti-fouling properties, with significantly reduced fouling degrees compared to control TFC membranes [274]. In another recent work, Azadi et al. [275] also focused on the preparation of a double-skinned ceramic membrane with an alumina substrate and a TiO2 active layer, a first-of-its-kind application in the FO filtration process. By optimizing fabrication parameters such as sol aging, calcination temperature, and dip time, their achieved membrane improved salt rejection and water flux. However, water flux decreased when using groundwater due to ions and ionic compositions, demanding further process optimization and evaluation of long-term fouling performance. These studies highlight novel approaches to enhance TFC-FO membrane performance in water treatment applications [275].

In summary, the double-skinned TFC-FO membrane demonstrates robust antifouling capabilities by enhancing the surface hydrophilicity and concurrently reducing the surface roughness of the PA selective layer. This is achieved through the adoption of a porous substrate characterized by a smooth surface, interconnected pores, and finger-like macro-pores, thus mitigating ICP during the FO process. However, the incorporation of a second skin layer may introduce potential drawbacks. It could elevate water transport resistance and consequently lower water permeation flux. Moreover, this design modification may escalate production costs and operational complexity when compared to conventional single-layer TFC-FO membranes, as for the aforementioned advancements. This complexity arises from the inevitability of additional fabrication steps and materials. Therefore, while the double-skinned configuration offers notable advantages in terms of antifouling performance and ICP mitigation, careful consideration must be given to its potential drawbacks and overall feasibility in practical applications within the context of cost-effectiveness and process simplicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Membrane testing methods for TFC-based FO membranes
ce:section.ce:para: For the effective application of TFC membranes in FO water treatment processes, it is essential to evaluate their performance through comprehensive membrane testing methods. These methods can include, but not limited, mechanical strength tests, feed/draw solution temperature effects, and various parametric analyses. Understanding these testing methods helps to optimize membrane properties and operational conditions for enhanced water flux, selectivity, and long-term durability.

Mechanical strength is a critical parameter for evaluating the robustness and durability of TFC-based FO membranes [274,275]. It is particularly important for ensuring that membranes can handle sudden pressure surges or variations without failure. Burst pressure tests are conducted by gradually increasing the hydraulic pressure on the membrane until it fails, and the pressure at the point of failure is recorded [276,277]. Tensile strength test determines the membrane's ability to withstand tensile forces. A sample of the membrane is clamped at both ends and subjected to increasing tensile force until it breaks. The maximum force applied before breaking provides a measure of the membrane's tensile strength. High tensile strength indicates that the membrane can resist tearing and deformation under operational conditions [276,278]. In addition, the elongation at Break evaluates the membrane's elasticity and flexibility by measuring the extent to which the membrane can be stretched before it breaks. Membranes with higher elongation at break values are better able to accommodate mechanical stresses and strains during operation, which is important for applications with dynamic or fluctuating operating conditions [276,279]. Idarraga-Mora et al.[278] investigated the mechanical behavior of commercial TFC membranes using aforementioned test, focusing on how strain affects their water and salt transport properties. The findings helped to model design guidelines for creating more effective membranes and modules for pressurized osmotic processes, such as PRO.

The temperature of the feed and draw solutions significantly affects the performance of TFC-based FO membranes. Temperature influences several key factors, including water flux, solute rejection, and membrane stability. Higher temperatures generally increase water flux across the membrane due to the reduction in water viscosity and the enhancement of diffusivity. However, excessively high temperatures can compromise membrane integrity by accelerating the degradation of the PA layer in TFC membranes [47,280]. Temperature variations can also impact fouling behavior [47]. Higher temperatures may exacerbate organic and biological fouling due to the increased metabolic activity of microorganisms and the enhanced adsorption of organic matter. Conversely, lower temperatures can reduce fouling rates but may also decrease water flux [281]. Thermal stability tests assess the membrane's performance across a range of temperatures [47,280]. Membranes are subjected to varying feed and draw solution temperatures, and their flux, rejection rates, and structural integrity are monitored over time. This helps determine the optimal operating temperature range for a given FO membrane [106,282]. Han et al. [99] investigated the influence of feed and draw solution temperatures on water flux and reverse solute flux. Their observations showed that increasing the temperature from 20 °C to 40 °C led to a 56% increase in water flux of TFC-FO membranes, but also resulted in a 76% increase in reverse solute flux.

Parametric analysis involves evaluating the effects of various operational parameters on the performance of TFC-based FO membranes [283]. These analyses help optimize membrane operation and improve process efficiency. The concentration gradient between the feed and draw solutions is the primary driving force in FO processes. Parametric studies assess how variations in the concentration of the draw solution (e.g., NaCl, MgSO₄) affect water flux and solute rejection [281,283]. High concentration gradients typically enhance water flux, but they may also increase the RSD and CP effects [14]. Also, crossflow velocity affects the rate of fouling and concentration polarization on the membrane surface. Increasing cross-flow velocity can reduce the ECP and fouling by sweeping away accumulated solutes and particulates from the membrane surface [16,17]. Parametric studies involving different crossflow velocities help identify the optimal flow conditions that minimize fouling and maximize flux [284]. Although FO processes do not require hydraulic pressure for water transport, understanding the effects of low-pressure conditions can be important for integrated FO systems (e.g., FO-RO hybrid systems). Parametric studies on hydraulic pressure impact help determine the threshold pressures that TFC membranes can withstand without compromising performance or integrity [284,285].

Long-term stability tests evaluate the durability and performance consistency of TFC-based FO membranes over extended periods of operation [286–288]. These tests are essential for understanding how membranes behave under real-world conditions and how their degradation over time [288]. Membranes are subjected to prolonged filtration cycles, simulating continuous operational conditions to assess changes in water flux, solute rejection, and fouling propensity over time [5,289]. Continuous operation tests help identify potential degradation mechanisms and inform strategies for improving membrane durability [289,290]. On a separate point, cyclic tests involve alternating between operational and cleaning cycles to evaluate membrane performance recovery after fouling and chemical cleaning [85,288]. These tests assess the resilience of the membrane material to repeated cleaning procedures, which is critical for maintaining performance in industrial applications [5]. As such, Wang et al. [291] studied the long-term irreversible fouling which found to pose a significant challenge to the sustained use of TFC-PA-FO membranes, particularly in processes such as wastewater treatment. Proper chemical cleaning protocols are crucial for maintaining the membrane's performance over time. The study demonstrated that exposure to certain cleaning agents, such as Alconox, caused irreversible damage to the membranes, leading to long-term degradation of their properties. However, the use of a specific cleaning protocol—0.1% NaOH/0.1% SDS followed by acid cleaning with either 2% citric acid or 0.5% HCl—proved to be the most effective strategy for long-term membrane performance, ensuring better water flux recovery and extending membrane lifespan.

In summary, understanding and implementing appropriate membrane testing methods are crucial for optimizing the performance of TFC-based FO membranes in water treatment applications. Mechanical strength testing, temperature effects, parametric analysis, and long-term stability testing provide valuable insights into membrane behavior, guiding improvements in membrane design, material selection, and operational protocols.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of Performance of TFC FO membrane
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that more effective TFC FO membranes exhibit increased water flux (Jw), lower reverse solute flux (Js), and higher solute rejection percentages (R%). While these parameters are vital to assess, they alone do not offer a thorough insight into the membrane performance. Therefore, determining the three intrinsic membrane parameters, water permeability (A), solute permeability (B), and structural parameter (S) is also essential to offer a complete picture of membrane performance.

A variety of experimental methods have been employed to assess the performance parameters of membrane technology, yet a universally accepted procedure has not been developed [50]. These methods include the pressurized RO-FO technique, the ultra-low-pressure RO-FO method, and the non-pressurized FO strategy, which can be divided into single and two-stage processes. The widely utilized pressurized RO-FO method typically assesses the water and salt permeabilities (A and B parameters) of FO membranes by exerting hydraulic pressure through an RO system. At the same time, the S-parameter is derived from testing under FO conditions. Cath et al. [292] proposed a standardized protocol for testing high-permeability membranes via the RO-FO approach, advising a feed pressure of 125 psi (with an optimal 70 psi for highly permeable variants) along with a crossflow velocity of 0.25 m/s for both RO and FO testing procedures. They employed deionized water to determine the A parameter and a 2000 ppm NaCl solution to conduct rejection tests to ascertain the B parameter. Nonetheless, due to the thin and highly porous FO membrane supports, the high-pressure conditions typically applied in RO-FO methods may not be suitable for these particular types of membranes [292]. Alternatively, Huang et al. [293] recommended determining the A parameter with much lower pressures at 25 psi via an RO setup and measuring B and S parameters using a conventional FO method.

Tiraferri et al. [294] have expressed concerns that methods utilizing hydraulic pressure could fall short due to their complexity, potential membrane damage during RO testing pressures, and the dubious assumption that transport parameters (A and B) remain consistent in both RO and FO processes. This point of view is supported by the inherent differences in the driving forces of permeation between the two methods, which may lead to inaccurate determination of transport parameters. In light of this, Tiraferri et al. [294] introduced an alternative approach for analyzing the intrinsic transport and structural properties of FO membranes through a non-pressured, single-stage FO experiment conducted in AL-FS orientation. This approach involves altering the concentration of the draw solution in several stages (typically four), allowing for the reporting of water and solute flux data. These findings are then applied to refine parameters A, B, and S by employing non-linear regression to tune into FO transport equations [294]. When comparing findings from this proposed method against those acquired through the widely adopted RO-FO technique, notable variances were observed. These differences were mainly ascribed to the influence of hydraulic pressure during RO-FO tests on parameters A and B determination, which not only potentially changed membrane characteristics but also contributed to errors when contrasted with the single-stage FO method.

Given that FO membranes can operate in two distinct orientations, AL-FS (FO mode) and AL-DS (PRO mode), using a single-stage FO testing method to determine intrinsic parameters may lead to inconsistencies when different orientations are applied, potentially affecting the accuracy of the intrinsic values obtained. Additionally, it has been consistently observed that Jw and Js are higher in the AL-DS orientation compared to the AL-FS orientation. It is also worth mentioning that most studies focus on comparing flux efficiency between FO and PRO modes rather than examining the intrinsic properties of the membranes themselves [295]. In response, Kim et al. [50] have recommended a non-pressurized two-stage test covering both AL-FS and AL-DS modes, named the PRO-FO method. This approach divides the assessment of the A and B parameters in the initial stage from that of the S parameter in the subsequent stage, considering all possible CP effects with both orientations. During the first stage set in AL-DS orientation with DI water as feed to avoid concentrative ICP, parameter A is determined by measuring water flux across varying concentrations of NaCl draw solution. Parameter (B) is obtained by tracking salt concentration changes in the feed solution, which rise due to solute reverse flow from the draw solution [50]. Subsequently, during the second stage, under an AL-FS orientation, experimental water fluxes are gauged using different NaCl concentrations as the draw solution and DI as feed. The S parameter value is then computed from these measured water fluxes and permeability data acquired from the first stage [50]. They claim that producing significant water fluxes in the first stage is essential for obtaining an accurate value of (S) and avoiding distortions caused by residual effects. They believe this approach simplifies calculations and improves precision when determining the intrinsic parameters of FO membranes under realistic driving forces and typical operating conditions without applying hydraulic pressure.( Table 6)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of TFC-based FO membranes in novel fields
ce:section.ce:para: As previously reported, TFC membranes are a critical technology in FO-based water treatment applications, due to their superior water permeability and salt rejection capabilities [151,247]. These membranes are now being explored for use in novel fields such as Osmotic Membrane Bioreactors (OMBR), where their performance can be enhanced by incorporating electric fields and other complementary technologies [118]. As such, OMBR systems utilize FO membranes to treat and purify wastewater, enhancing the osmotic pressure differential to draw water through the membrane while retaining contaminants. However, challenges such as membrane fouling, and CP can limit performance [295,296]. Recent studies have indicated that applying an electric field to TFC-based FO membranes in OMBR systems can significantly mitigate these issues. Integrating an electric field across TFC-based FO membranes creates a localized electrostatic environment that can disrupt foulant adhesion and promote the detachment of fouling layers. Studies have shown that applying a moderate electric potential (e.g., 2.5 V) across the membrane can effectively reduce biofouling by generating reactive oxidative species such as chlorine, which degrade biofilm and microbial cells [296,297]. For instance, Wang et al. [298] demonstrated that TFC membranes modified with electrochemically conductive materials, such as CNT interlayers, could restore up to 85% of their original water flux under an applied voltage, highlighting the effectiveness of this approach in maintaining long-term membrane performance in OMBR applications.

In addition to using electric fields, several advanced technologies can be combined with TFC-based FO membranes to further enhance their performance in OMBR and other applications including: (a) nanocomposite modification: TFC-based FO membranes can be embedded with nanomaterials such as TiO2 nanoparticles, GO, or CNTs to enhance their properties [253,281,299]. These nanocomposite membranes have demonstrated improved water flux and salt rejection rates due to the increased hydrophilicity and reduced membrane resistance. For example, embedding TiO2 nanoparticles within TFC membranes can impart photocatalytic properties, enabling the degradation of organic contaminants under UV light exposure, thereby providing a self-cleaning function that reduces fouling [300]. (b) interlayer-modified TFC-FO membranes: Interlayers such as PDA, and MOFs have been introduced between the TFC membrane's selective layer and support layer to enhance mechanical strength, hydrophilicity, and antifouling properties [132,301]. Han et al. [239] demonstrated that TFC membranes modified with a PDA interlayer exhibited higher water permeability and maintained salt rejection, while also reducing fouling propensity by preventing the adhesion of foulants. (c) double-skinned TFC-FO membranes: The development of double-skinned TFC membranes, which include two selective layers, has been explored to tackle ICP and improve fouling resistance [263,265]. Wang et al. [266] developed a double-skinned TFC membrane with a dual selective layer structure that effectively minimized ICP while maintaining high water permeability and salt rejection. This design allows for better management of solute diffusion and water flux, making it particularly useful in OMBR applications where long-term operational stability is critical. These membranes’ technologies can show potential for applications such as OMBR, where maintaining high water flux and salt rejection is critical over long-term operation.

To fully realize the potential of TFC-based FO membranes in novel applications such as OMBR, further research should focus on the following: (a) Optimization of electric field parameters: Detailed studies should investigate the optimal electric field parameters (voltage, current density, frequency) for different membrane types to maximize antifouling effects without compromising membrane integrity or performance [302]. (b) Hybrid systems development: developing hybrid TFC-based FO membranes that integrate multiple enhancement strategies, such as interlayer modifications, nanocomposite incorporation, and electric field application could offer significant improvements in terms of fouling resistance, water flux, and operational stability [237]. (c) Scale-up and economic feasibility: Efforts should be directed towards scaling up these advanced membrane technologies from laboratory to industrial-scale applications (see Section 2.7.1), ensuring these should remain economically viable and sustainable while delivering enhanced performance [284,286].

The present state of desalination and water purification owes a lot to PA TFC membranes. PSF and PES are likely to continue as the preferred polymer materials for the support layer due to their outstanding chemical properties and sufficient mechanical durability. This paper reviews recent advancements in PA TFC membranes for FO processes, focusing on various modification methods and their impact on membrane performance in water purification applications. The development of new, durable, high-performing membranes is crucial for achieving sustainable FO applications. FO membranes that are chemically and mechanically stable and feature minimal ICP, high water flux, and negligible RSF are essential for optimizing the efficiency of the FO water purification process. TFC membranes are pivotal in FO technology due to their unique structural configuration and high performance. These membranes typically feature a meticulously engineered thin skin layer designed for optimal salt rejection, along with a highly porous support layer that facilitates exceptional water transport. TFC FO membranes require a durable yet thin substrate along with a highly efficient active layer that offers excellent water permeability and selectivity. This can contribute to making FO a viable and competitive process for desalination and water treatment applications. The interplay between these two layers (the skin and the support) is essential for achieving the desired efficiency in TFC-FO applications. However, the inherent hydrophobicity and negatively charged nature of the top PA layer in TFC membranes, which has a distinct rough ridge-and-valley structure can pose significant challenges, such as membrane fouling, which compromises performance. To mitigate this issue, various surface modification techniques, such as LbL, blending nanomaterials/additives, and surface grafting, have been explored. Among them, blending nanomaterials and additives into the PA layer is preferred over LbL or functionalized TFC membranes since it could be easier to manufacture, with fewer preparation steps involved.

Enhancing the efficiency of water transport in conventional TFC membranes is frequently hindered by the properties of the support layer. Nonetheless, this issue can potentially be mitigated by utilizing a support layer that exhibits high hydrophilicity and higher porosity, comprised of smaller pores with a greater pore number density. These attributes help reduce the membrane's structural parameter and ICP, thereby significantly enhancing the flux stability and separation performance of the TFC FO membrane. Additionally, the formation of PA rejection layers is greatly affected by substrate properties. It is clear that substrates with larger pores might decrease the confinement effect, allowing polyamide to penetrate the pores in some cases, resulting in less pronounced "ridge-and-valley" roughness features. Several modification techniques have been applied to alter the membrane support layer, with the blending method involving nanomaterials and pore forming being the most commonly used strategy because of its relative ease of operation. Interlayer membrane is another modified technique that has shown great promise in improving the performance of TFC FO membranes. A strategically designed interlayer between the PA layer and the porous substrate effectively manages the membrane structure, leading to a thinner, defect-free PA selective layer. Additionally, the double-skinned TFC FO membrane, with its dual dense rejection layers, has successfully reduced ICP and fouling, even though it may result in decreased water flux. Various fabrication and modification techniques have been employed to develop TFC FO membranes and significant progress has been made in water treatment applications. Nevertheless, the increased complexity and potentially higher costs of these techniques must be carefully considered in relation to their benefits for practical application.

Finally, despite advances in experimental research aimed at modifying TFC FO membranes, their commercialization and economically feasible implementation remain challenging. Currently, more research should focus on scaling up the fabrication of TFC-FO membranes for practical applications. The challenge lies in scaling up the modification of TFC-FO membranes with excellent permeability and selectivity from lab-scale to mass production through cost-effective methods. One potential solution is to develop a more scalable and sustainable approach, considering the relatively low cost and existing production lines used for commercial membranes. Another concern is that the evaluation of FO membrane performance is not yet universal due to inconsistent testing conditions and protocols across different studies, making it hard to compare the performance of various fabricated membranes. Therefore, establishing a standard method for testing FO membranes could lead to consistent and comparable results across different manufactured membranes.
Hamza Ennaji Almansouri: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Mohamed Edokali: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Mazrul N. Abu Seman: Writing – review & editing.
